  • SHACL (not covered in detail in this series): "This field must exist," "This value must be a number." Define the rules once, and they are checked automatically as data is loaded. AI answer quality follows from data quality.
  • SPARQL_TABLE: Query the KG with one line in the SQL Console, for example SELECT * FROM SPARQL_TABLE('...'). No separate SPARQL endpoint is required.
  • Inference (not covered in detail in this series): Derive relationships through RDFS/OWL rules without explicit triples. Declare Manager = Leader in the ontology, and Alice (a Manager) is automatically queryable as a Leader without modifying the data.

These four features form the core of HANA Cloud KGE. How each one is applied in practice is covered in the corresponding post in this series.

What's Next

If this principle holds, the next question is how to actually build it.

Part 2-1 – Ontology Standards and Concepts covers the standards and concepts: what an ontology is, Class vs Instance, and how the W3C standards (Direct Mapping and R2RML) define the translation from relational data to RDF. Part 2-2 – Designing the Ontology for SAP Data then walks through the practical design decisions we made for SAP data, including what to place in the KG versus leave in SQL, Class Partition, the resulting ontology, and how we used AI to draft it. The two posts work as a pair, but Part 2-2 is also designed to stand on its own.

Stack: SAP BDC Data Products · HANA Cloud KGE · FastAPI · React · Claude (Anthropic / AI Core) Code: github.com/claudiopark86/sap-bdc-kge-agent-workshop

References

  • Ontology Development 101: A Guide to Creating Your First Ontology — Noy & McGuinness, Stanford 2001. The standard introduction to ontology design methodology. The source for concepts covered throughout this series, including Competency Questions, Class vs Instance, and iterative design. https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf
  • Harnessing Half a Century of Knowledge: SAP's Journey of Enriching APIs with Metadata — SAP Community Blog. SAP's evolved metadata strategy and ODM vision. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/harnessing-half-a-century-of-knowledge-sap-s-journey-of-enriching-apis-with/ba-p/13578364

entry.title: Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 2-1: Ontology Standards and Concepts
entry.content:
This blog is part of a blog series on building AI Agents with SAP BDC (Business Data Cloud) Data Products and SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine:

  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 1: Why Knowledge Graph for SAP Data
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 2-1: Ontology Standards and Concepts
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 2-2: Designing the Ontology for SAP Data 
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 3: Data Pipeline from BDC to HANA Cloud KGE
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 4: Building an Agent with SPARQL and SQL
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 5: Lessons Learned

Enterprise systems are vast, with thousands of tables, hundreds of business processes, and countless APIs, all interconnected in ways only domain experts fully grasp. For AI Agents to be genuinely useful in this environment, they need to understand not just what the data is, but how it all connects: which Product belongs to which Sales Order, which WorkCenter is located in which Plant, which Supplier serves which material. This understanding does not come from the data itself. It needs a dedicated semantic layer that makes business meaning explicit and machine-readable. Knowledge Graphs provide exactly that layer. SAP HANA Cloud now offers a native Knowledge Graph Engine to build it on top of SAP BDC Data Products. This series walks through the full journey: from why this layer matters, to how to design and build it, to how an AI Agent uses it to answer real business questions.

This post covers the concepts and standards of ontology design. It explains what an ontology is, why it works well with LLMs, what the seven-step methodology from Stanford's Ontology Development 101 proposes, and what the W3C standards (Direct Mapping and R2RML) define when translating relational data into RDF. This is the most theoretically detailed post in the series, providing the vocabulary and references that underpin the design decisions in later posts.

💡 Reading guide. This post goes deep into theory and W3C standards. If you would rather see the practical SAP BDC decisions right away, you can skip ahead to Part 2-2 – Designing the Ontology for SAP Data. Part 2-2 is structured so that you can follow it without reading this post to the end, and you can return here when you want the background.

Table of contents:

  1. What is an Ontology
    • 1.1 Core concepts
    • 1.2 Class vs Instance
    • 1.3 Property: connecting nodes and carrying values (ObjectProperty / DatatypeProperty / Domain & Range)
    • 1.4 Design principles (IRI, Competency Questions)
    • 1.5 Labels, multilingual text, and synonyms (rdfs:label, language tags, skos:altLabel)
  2. The Seven Steps of Ontology 101: the process we followed
  3. From RDB to RDF: what the W3C standards define
    • 3.1 Class definition
    • 3.2 Instance data identification
    • 3.3 Data attribute representation
    • 3.4 Relationship representation
    • Combined example
  4. Three domain decisions we made on top of the standards

What this post covers

This post addresses the theory and standards within the series. It introduces what an ontology is, the seven-step methodology from Stanford's Ontology Development 101, and the two W3C standards (Direct Mapping and R2RML) that define how relational data is mapped to RDF, including what they automate and where domain decisions are required.

This material is the background knowledge for the decisions that follow (Class Partition, KG vs SQL classification, Relationship SQL Views). The decisions themselves are covered from Part 2-2 onward, so if you want to move directly to practical decisions, you can skip ahead to Part 2-2.

One practical use: the content of this post can also be provided to an LLM as a prompt. The seven-step methodology from Ontology 101, the W3C mapping rules (Table → Class, Row → Instance, and so on), and the conventions for defining classes and properties using RDFS/OWL vocabulary. Feeding this standards guide together with your domain information to a generative model produces ontology drafts that are much closer to W3C standards. This is what determines the quality of the "drafting with AI" approach we cover in Part 2-2.

1. What is an Ontology

1.1 Core concepts

Before building a Knowledge Graph, it is important to understand what an ontology is. Stated simply:

An ontology is an explicit definition of "what kinds of things exist in this world, and how they are connected."

For an ontology in the supply chain domain:

  • These kinds of things exist: WorkCenter, Product, Supplier, Plant
  • They are connected like this: WorkCenters produce Products, Suppliers provide Products

An ontology file is this information written in a machine-readable format (RDF/OWL).

Why build one? Ontology Development 101 puts it this way:

"Developing an ontology is akin to defining a set of data and their structure for other programs to use."

The goal is not the ontology itself. The goal is to define the structure and vocabulary of data that an AI Agent or application will consume. At its core, the value is common understanding: making it possible for humans, LLMs, and software agents to interpret the same concepts through the same vocabulary.

This is where SAP BDC and One Domain Model come in. ODM already defines a standard vocabulary across S/4HANA, including WorkCenter, SalesOrder, and Supplier. When you build an ontology, you use this vocabulary directly. The LLM already knows what these words mean. The KG is structured with the same vocabulary. The Agent writes SPARQL using the same vocabulary.

The "common understanding" that Ontology 101 advocates is, in the case of SAP data, already provided by One Domain Model. Half of ontology design is done before you begin.

# Ontology example (Turtle format)
bdc:WorkCenter a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "WorkCenter" ;
    rdfs:comment "Production work center" .

bdc:Product a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Product" ;
    rdfs:comment "Material/product" .

bdc:producesProduct a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain bdc:WorkCenter ;
    rdfs:range  bdc:Product ;
    rdfs:label  "produces product" .

1.2 Class vs Instance: the first distinction

When first encountering ontologies, the difference between Class and Instance is often confusing. This distinction is the starting point for the entire KG structure.

  • Class = concept. "There is a kind of thing called WorkCenter."
  • Instance = actual data. "Z_ASM3 is an actual WorkCenter that exists."

By analogy with a BDC Virtual Table:

KG concept ExampleVirtual Table analogy
Classbdc:WorkCenterTable schema (column definitions)
Instancebdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3One row in the table

This is why there are two files:

  • bdc_dp_ontology.ttl: class and relationship definitions (schema)
  • bdc_dp_instances.ttl: actual WorkCenter, Product, and Supplier data

There are as many instances as there are rows in the Virtual Table. With the demo data containing 129 WorkCenters, there are 129 instances.

1.3 Property: connecting nodes and carrying values

If Class and Instance describe "what exists," Property describes "how things are connected and what values they hold." RDF/OWL distinguishes two types of property:

TypeValue form Example
owl:ObjectProperty (relationship)IRI of another nodebdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3 bdc:producesProduct bdc:Product_MZ-FG-C900
owl:DatatypeProperty (attribute)literal (string / number / date)bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3 bdc:workCenterName "Z_ASM3"

💡 If this is confusing: think of DatatypeProperty as "a regular column that does not lead to another table." A relational column such as WORK_CENTER.WorkCenterName, which simply holds a row's attribute, becomes a DatatypeProperty like bdc:workCenterName in the KG. Conversely, ObjectProperty resembles "a foreign key that points to another table." A column such as WORK_CENTER.PlantID, which points to another row, becomes an ObjectProperty like bdc:locatedAt in the KG.

This distinction is what makes a graph a graph. With only DatatypeProperties, each node would carry its own attributes but would not link to other nodes. They would be closer to isolated records than a connected graph. The moment ObjectProperty connects a node to another node, the records become a graph, and SPARQL can traverse chains such as "WorkCenter → Product → Customer."

In Turtle, both are declared with the same rdf:type syntax:

bdc:producesProduct a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain bdc:WorkCenter ;
    rdfs:range  bdc:FinishedGood .

bdc:workCenterName a owl:DatatypeProperty ;
    rdfs:domain bdc:WorkCenter ;
    rdfs:range  xsd:string .

Domain & Range: applying type constraints to properties

Each property can carry type constraints through rdfs:domain and rdfs:range:

  • rdfs:domain: the class expected to appear as the subject of this property. "producesProduct is intended to be used with WorkCenter as its subject."
  • rdfs:range:
    • For ObjectProperty: the class that can serve as the object. "The value of producesProduct is a FinishedGood."
    • For DatatypeProperty: the XSD type of the value. xsd:string, xsd:integer, xsd:date, and so on.

Without these modeling guardrails, SPARQL queries may return invalid results, for example, a Supplier producing a product, or a Plant being produced. Domain and Range help both humans and LLMs use the intended classes when writing SPARQL queries.

Practical guidance (Ontology 101):

  • Use the most general class for Domain and Range. If you want to include every Product, use bdc:Product instead of bdc:FinishedGood.
  • Do not make the range too broad either (setting it to owl:Thing carries no meaning).
  • All subclasses are automatically included. If the range is bdc:Product, its subclasses (FinishedGood/SemiFinished/RawMaterial) are included as well.

Edge case: DatatypeProperty vs ObjectProperty

There are cases where it is not obvious whether a value should be a "plain string" or "a separate node." For example, the country of a supplier:

  • If a simple value is sufficient → owl:DatatypeProperty (bdc:country "DE"^^xsd:string)
  • If multiple systems or multiple classes reference the same country, or if the country itself needs a label or description → model the country as a separate node and use owl:ObjectProperty (bdc:locatedInCountry bdc:Country_DE)

The latter is the pattern that turns a KG into an "extensible asset," but for simple representations, a DatatypeProperty is sufficient. This is a domain decision.

1.4 Design principles: IRI, Competency Questions

💡 IRI notation. In RDF, every resource (class, property, instance) is identified by an IRI (Internationalized Resource Identifier). You can think of it as a URL. The two are nearly the same for this discussion. Originally, it appears in the full URL form, such as <http://sap.com/bdc/WorkCenter>.

Why include the domain (URL)? To prevent collisions when the same name is used with different meanings across domains. For example, the Customer in one system and the Customer in another system may represent different concepts; embedding the domain in the IRI makes the two resources distinguishable. Both can be loaded into the same KG without collision, and when two KGs are later integrated, you can decide explicitly how to map them.

Writing the full URL every time becomes verbose, so Turtle allows prefix declarations as a shorthand. This series uses bdc: as the single prefix for the SAP BDC domain.

@prefix bdc:  <http://sap.com/bdc/> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix owl:  <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> .
@prefix xsd:  <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

From here on, bdc:WorkCenter (= <http://sap.com/bdc/WorkCenter>) and bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3 (= <http://sap.com/bdc/WorkCenter_Z_ASM3>) can be written in this short form. Classes, relationships, properties, and instances are distinguished by naming conventions:


TypeConventionExample
ClassPascalCasebdc:WorkCenter, bdc:Customer
Relationship (ObjectProperty)camelCase, verb phrasebdc:producesProduct, bdc:hasAddress
Attribute (DatatypeProperty)camelCasebdc:country, bdc:customerID
InstanceClassName_identifierbdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3, bdc:Customer_0001000010

This convention follows the practice of community-standard RDF vocabularies such as schema.org and FOAF. Most code examples in this series use the abbreviated form. The W3C standard examples in Section 3 (<People/ID=7>) use full IRIs without prefixes. Both forms represent the same IRI notation.

For extension: separate prefixes by domain. We start with the single SAP BDC domain, so a single bdc: prefix is enough. As the KG expands into other domains, the standard RDF convention is to introduce a separate prefix per domain. For example, when integrating data from another SAP product, or external systems such as logistics information or third-party master data, those would be placed under a different prefix and domain URL so the two vocabularies do not collide. Rather than packing everything into bdc:, prefixes are added along domain boundaries. This is the prefix-level realization of the "KG as an extensible asset" point emphasized in Part 1 Section 6.

📖 Terminology note: In Description Logic and KG literature, the two layers are called T-Box (Terminological Box, definitions of classes and relationships, the schema) and A-Box (Assertion Box, the actual instance facts). The two TTL files in our project correspond exactly to these layers: bdc_dp_ontology.ttl is the T-Box, and bdc_dp_instances.ttl is the A-Box.

Competency Questions: write the questions before designing

Ontology Development 101 (Noy & McGuinness, Stanford 2001) emphasizes one core principle:

"Before designing an ontology, write down the questions this KG must answer."

These are called Competency Questions. Defining the questions first determines naturally which classes and relationships are needed.

The Competency Questions for our project:

QuestionWhat is needed
"What finished goods does Z_ASM3 produce?"

bdc:WorkCenter

bdc:producesProduct

bdc:FinishedGood

"What is the per-customer revenue exposure for these finished goods?"SQL (No KG required. Transactional data)
"What products does this supplier provide?"

bdc:Supplier

bdc:fromSupplier

 bdc:forProduct

 bdc:PurchasingSourceList

"Which finished goods does this raw material go into?"bdc:hasComponent (Product → Product)

Once the questions are written, three things are determined:

  1. Which classes are needed: the nouns in the question.
  2. Which relationships are needed: the verbs in the question.
  3. What goes into the KG versus what stays in SQL: relationships requiring SPARQL traversal go into the KG; numeric aggregation stays in SQL.

These questions later serve as SPARQL test cases. After loading, if SPARQL returns the correct answer for each question, the ontology is well designed.

1.5 Labels, multilingual text, and synonyms: rdfs:label, language tags, skos:altLabel

💡 Reading note. This section is longer because labels, comments, and synonyms directly affect how well an AI Agent interprets KG results. If you already know RDFS/SKOS annotations, you can skim this section and continue with Section 2.

rdfs:label and rdfs:comment: the basics

In a KG, attaching rdfs:label to every Class, Property, and Instance is standard practice. Without labels, SPARQL results return only URIs such as bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3, which are hard for an LLM to interpret. rdfs:comment adds a short description that helps the LLM understand the role of each class or property when ontology context is provided. Practical authoring rules are covered in Part 2-2.

Multilingual: language tags

Attaching a language tag to the same property supports multiple languages at once.

bdc:WorkCenter a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label   "WorkCenter"@en ,
                 "작업장"@ko ;
    rdfs:comment "Production work center."@en ,
                 "생산 작업장."@ko .

In SPARQL, FILTER(LANG(?label) = "ko") selects the desired language. This works because RDF stores "작업장"@ko and "WorkCenter"@en as distinct literals from the start. The language tag is part of the value itself, and SPARQL's LANG() function reads it. This works by default, with no additional configuration required.

Synonyms: reusing skos:altLabel

rdfs:label provides a human-readable name, but it does not distinguish between preferred and alternative names. In natural language search or GraphRAG contexts, the same node must be discoverable whether the user searches for "supplier" or "vendor." For this, a way to represent synonyms is required.

This is where we reuse SKOS (Simple Knowledge Organization System, W3C 2009 Recommendation) and its skos:altLabel. SKOS is originally a standard for classification systems and controlled vocabularies. Its central concept, skos:Concept, represents "a unit of thought," such as a classification value or a category item. This is different from an OWL Class or instance such as bdc:WorkCenter in our KG.

The three SKOS labels:

  • skos:prefLabel: preferred form (one per language is recommended)
  • skos:altLabel: synonyms, abbreviations, and notation variants (no count limit)
  • skos:hiddenLabel: labels indexed for search but not displayed (typos, deprecated forms, and so on)

All three are sub-properties (rdfs:subPropertyOf) of rdfs:label. That is, attaching prefLabel already implies rdfs:label.

skos:altLabel does not enforce rdfs:domain of skos:Concept. The SKOS specification intentionally omits a domain so that altLabel can be attached to resources of any type. Using skos:altLabel on a class or a skos:Concept, is a borrowed use of the property.

TargetMeaningExampleskos:altLabel
skos:ConceptA classification value being referencedCategories such as "Domestic Supplier," "Overseas Supplier"Native use
owl:ClassA set with members"There is a kind called Supplier"Borrowed
InstanceAn actual entityReal Suppliers such as "USSU-VSF01," "USSU-VSF02"Borrowed
bdc:Supplier (Class)
    ├── USSU-VSF01  →  hasRole  →  c_domestic   (Domestic Supplier)
    └── USSU-VSF02  →  hasRole  →  c_overseas   (Overseas Supplier)

c_domestic, c_overseas  →  skos:Concept (classification values)

Domestic/overseas classification can also be expressed via a DatatypeProperty. skos:Concept is used when the classification value itself must be modeled as an independent concept, for example when it is shared across multiple systems or requires its own label or description.

Borrowing on a class (the approach used in this project):

@prefix skos: <http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#> .

bdc:Supplier a owl:Class ;                # owl:Class — borrowed use
    rdfs:label    "Supplier"@en ,
                  "공급사"@ko ;            # preferred name (for display)
    skos:altLabel "공급업체"@ko ,          # synonym (for search)
                  "벤더"@ko ,
                  "Vendor"@en ;
    rdfs:comment  "A counterparty that supplies materials or services."@en .

The same pattern applies to instances:

ex:successFactors a ex:Product ;            # Instance — also borrowed
    rdfs:label    "SAP SuccessFactors"@en ;
    skos:altLabel "SuccessFactors"@en ;
    skos:altLabel "SFSF"@en ;
    skos:altLabel "석세스팩터스"@ko .        # Korean notation

In this project, the pattern is applied only at the class level. Instance-level synonyms would require leveraging source columns (Virtual Table) or building a separate mapping, which is outside the scope of these examples.

The line not to cross is this: if synonyms are needed, do not change the resource type to skos:Concept. That would be a misuse. bdc:Supplier remains an owl:Class, ex:successFactors remains an instance, and only skos:altLabel is layered on top.

To search across the preferred name and synonyms in a single SPARQL query, use property path alternation (|) :

SELECT ?class ?label WHERE {
  ?class a owl:Class .
  ?class rdfs:label|skos:altLabel ?label .
  FILTER(CONTAINS(LCASE(?label), LCASE("vendor")))
}

📖 Standard basis: The SKOS Reference (W3C Recommendation, 2009) does not declare a domain on skos:altLabel, and presents graphs attaching labels to owl:Class as official examples. This is not an informal workaround but a legitimate use that the standard explicitly permits. https://www.w3.org/TR/skos-reference/

Can synonyms work without SKOS?

Yes. A synonym is ultimately just a triple, and can be expressed without SKOS. The important point is that SKOS does not "enable" synonyms in the first place.

The simplest approach is to define a custom property:

ex:successFactors a ex:Product ;
    rdfs:label "SAP SuccessFactors"@en ;
    ex:alias   "SFSF"@en, "SuccessFactors"@en, "석세스팩터스"@ko .

ex:alias is a custom property. Search works exactly the same way (?p rdfs:label|ex:alias ?label). For GraphRAG-style search alone, this is sufficient.

Alternatively, all variants can be packed into rdfs:label, and search will still work. The trade-off is that the distinction between preferred and alternative forms disappears, so "which one to show as the primary form in a UI" becomes ambiguous.

So what does SKOS provide in addition? All three approaches deliver the same "synonym search." The value of SKOS lies elsewhere:

 

 ex:alias(custom)  ⭕rdfs:labelpacked⭕skos:altLabel⭕
Synonym search⭕⭕⭕
Preferred/alternative role distinctionBuild it yourself❌⭕built in (pref/alt/hidden)
Standard (recognized by tools and people)❌custom property△⭕W3C
Cross-system alignment anchor❌❌⭕(mapping properties)

Borrowing skos:altLabel is not purely about future-proofing. Its value spans two time horizons:

  • Immediate value: as a standard, other tools and people immediately recognize it as "a synonym," and the role distinction (pref/alt/hidden) is predefined, so there is no need to reinvent the wheel.
  • Future value: when extending into cross-system mapping (skos:exactMatch and similar), the same vocabulary family is reused.

When to use what

What you need Approach
One preferred namerdfs:label
Synonyms, abbreviationsskos:altLabel
Multilingual preferred namerdfs:label "이름"@ko , "Name"@en
Multilingual synonymsskos:altLabel "벤더"@ko , "Vendor"@en
Classification or code systemskos:Concept + skos:prefLabel / skos:altLabel (out of scope for this example; extend when needed)

Typical situations where synonyms must be attached:

  • GraphRAG or natural language search: the same node must be discoverable regardless of how the user phrases the query.
  • Multilingual environments: a single concept must be searchable in both English and Korean.
  • System integration: terms from other systems must be marked as representing the same concept.

📖 Reference: The concepts covered in this section (Competency Questions, Class vs Instance, Domain & Range) are based on Ontology Development 101 (Noy & McGuinness, Stanford 2001), the standard introduction to ontology design. https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf

2. The Seven Steps of Ontology 101: The Process We Followed

The concepts covered in Section 1 (Class/Instance, Domain & Range, Competency Questions) all originate from Stanford's Ontology Development 101. This guide does not just enumerate concepts. It proposes a seven-step process for building an ontology.

We followed this seven-step process directly when designing the BDC Data Products-based KG. Before going into the practical work in the following sections, here is the high-level mapping.

The seven-step process

StepOriginal step MeaningMapping in our project
1Determine the domain and scopeDetermine domain and scope (with Competency Questions)"How far does the impact spread when Z_ASM3 stops?" (the four CQs from Section 1)
2Consider reusing existing ontologiesEvaluate reuse of existing ontologiesOne Domain Model: standard vocabulary (WorkCenter, Supplier, Product) used as-is
3Enumerate important termsEnumerate important termsExploring entities and column names from BDC Virtual Tables (Part 3 Section 1)
4Define classes and class hierarchyDefine classes and the class hierarchyMasterData/StructuralNode upper layer + six classes (including the Product partition in Part 2-2)
5Define properties of classesDefine the properties of classesproducesProduct, locatedAt, hasComponent, and other ObjectProperties
6Define facets of propertiesDefine property facets (cardinality, value type)Domain/Range constraints, owl:disjointWith (Part 2-2)
7Create instancesCreate instancesPart 3 Section 5 (Relationship Views → TTL triples)

If you remember one thing: iteration is the essence

The point that Ontology 101 emphasizes most is that "it is never completed in one pass."

"Designing an ontology is necessarily an iterative process."

Attempting to model an entire domain in one pass is rarely realistic:

  • Scope is hard to bound: the boundary of "how far to model" becomes unclear.
  • Completion is hard to verify: without a defined test scope, the quality benchmark is unclear.
  • Project milestones are hard to set: schedules and deliverables blur, making productization difficult.

After one full pass through the seven steps, you have the minimum KG required to answer the first CQ. When a new CQ arrives, you return to Step 1 and run the cycle again.

To borrow the book's phrasing:

"Develop the ontology incrementally, one competency question at a time."

Class Partition (Part 2-2), the Relationship SQL View (Part 3 Section 3), and the decision to leave Customer in SQL (Part 2-2) were not produced in the first iteration. They were solutions to problems discovered while validating the CQs.

📖 Source: Noy, N. F., & McGuinness, D. L. (2001). Ontology Development 101: A Guide to Creating Your First Ontology. Stanford KSL Technical Report. https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf

3. From RDB to RDF: What the W3C Standards Define

ℹ️ Where this section sits: If Section 1 and Section 2 dealt with the ontology (schema) itself, this section covers its counterpart, the instance (data) translation standard. The two together make a KG. What Section 2 calls Step 7 (Create Instances) in the Ontology 101 process is exactly what the W3C standardized here.

So far we have covered "what an ontology is" and "how to design one." When data already lives in a relational database, as with SAP BDC, the question of how to translate it into RDF triples was standardized by the W3C in 2012.

The automatic translation by the standard alone is not sufficient. SAP data often involves business relationships that span multiple tables, that exist without FK declarations, or that require a single table to be split into multiple classes. A more flexible approach is needed. For this reason, the W3C standardized both automatic translation (Direct Mapping) and custom mapping (R2RML) together.

📐 R2RML produces instance data, not ontology

R2RML is frequently misunderstood at first encounter. "Does this produce an ontology, or instance data?" The answer is instance data. R2RML is a file that contains only transformation rules such as "map this row of this table as an instance of which class, and map this column as which property." The class definitions themselves (bdc:Supplier is a class with the following properties) live in a separate ontology file, and R2RML simply references them.

Three files form a useful mental model:

FileWhat it containsExample
Ontology fileClass and property definitions (the blueprint)bdc:Supplier a owl:Class
R2RML mapping fileThe rule "take a row from this table (or the result of this SQL query) and convert it to instance data according to that blueprint"Row of SUPPLIER_MASTER = instance of bdc:Supplier
Instance dataThe actual data triplesbdc:Supplier_S001 a bdc:Supplier ; bdc:name "ACME"

There are two standards:

 

 Direct Mapping R2RML
Translation methodAutomatic, based on schemaMapping file authored manually
Class / relationship / attribute namesTable and column names used as-is (Person_table, Person_table#name)Free to assign business vocabulary (bdc:Supplier, bdc:hasComponent, bdc:supplierName)
When to useWhen every table maps cleanly to a single class and FKs accurately express all business relationshipsWhen one table needs to be split into multiple classes (for example, splitting MaterialType into FinishedGood/SemiFinished/RawMaterial), when FKs are missing or relationships span multi-hop chains, or when ABAP abbreviations (EKKO, KNA1) need to be replaced with business vocabulary (PurchaseOrder, Customer)

Direct Mapping provides the baseline automatic mapping from relational data to RDF, while R2RML provides a customizable mapping language for cases where the default mapping is not sufficient. Below, each of the four mapping types is presented side by side as "what Direct Mapping does automatically" versus "what R2RML / our project actually did."

📝 Note: Our project did not use an R2RML engine directly. We generated triples using Python scripts, but the principles are identical to those defined by R2RML.

A preview of R2RML standard vocabulary

Sections 3.1–3.4 below cover the four mapping types. Before entering the detail, here is a preview of how our SQL View and Python approach maps to R2RML standard vocabulary, limited to the three core constructs. As you read Sections 3.1–3.4, recognizing "this corresponds to that R2RML construct" is sufficient.

R2RML constructWhat the standard definesStep in Section 3
rr:tableNameDesignates a single table as the mapping input sourceSection 3.1: single table → single class
rr:sqlQueryDesignates an arbitrary SQL SELECT result as the mapping input sourceSection 3.1: multiple tables JOINed → single class (for example, SUPPLIER_MASTER + DETAIL → bdc:Supplier) / Section 3.4: multi-hop relationship View
rr:joinConditionJoins two tables inside a RefObjectMap to produce relationship triplesSection 3.4: FK join is precomputed inside the View; two IRIs are exposed together in a single row

This table pairs only "what (Instance Data)" and "how connected (Relationship)." The IRI construction in Section 3.2 Instance Data Identification (rr:subjectMap) and the column → attribute mapping in Section 3.3 Data Attribute (rr:predicateObjectMap of literal form) are straightforward and not given separate rows.

Inverting this view yields the picture of using the DB itself as the mapping engine, without a separate R2RML processing engine. When a View exposes every necessary column in a single row (the subject's ID and the relationship target's ID), the JOIN that an R2RML engine would perform inside its mapping is already done by the database. The output (relationship triples) is the same.

📌 The Relationship SQL View pattern in Part 3 is precisely this picture. The HANA SQL Views (KGR_*) precompute multi-hop JOINs, and the TTL generation script reads those Views to write relationship triples directly. The transformation semantics defined by the standard are followed exactly, without a separate mapping engine.

ℹ️ Note: another mode. The discussion above assumes the "materialize relationship triples ahead of time and write them into the graph" approach. The standard defines another mode as well. Instead of materializing triples in advance, joinConditions are translated into SQL JOINs on the fly each time a SPARQL query arrives. This series follows the materialized approach.

3.1 Class definition

W3C Direct Mapping

One table becomes one class. Each row is automatically translated into an instance triple of that class.

<People/ID=7> rdf:type <People> .

However, the class definition itself is not generated. Direct Mapping outputs only instance triples. The declaration that <People> is a class must be written separately.

<People> rdf:type rdfs:Class ;
         rdfs:label "Person" ;
         rdfs:comment "Business entity representing a person" .

R2RML / our project

Direct Mapping's "1 Table = 1 Class" fits simple relational databases well, but real SAP data is different. Class and Table are not a 1:1 correspondence.

1 Table → N Classes (partition): when a single table contains multiple kinds of entities. The BDC Product table is split into three classes based on the MaterialType column.

BDC_DP.PRODUCT (single table)
    MaterialType = FERT  →  bdc:FinishedGood
    MaterialType = HALB  →  bdc:SemiFinished
    MaterialType = ROH   →  bdc:RawMaterial

N Tables → 1 Class (consolidation): when multiple tables form a single business concept. WorkCenter and WorkCenterCapacity are separate tables, but in the KG they are represented by a single class bdc:WorkCenter.

"Which data belongs to which class" is determined by Competency Questions and domain decisions, not by table schema. Class Partition is covered in detail in Part 2-2.

In our project, bdc_dp_ontology.ttl is the file that holds the class definitions.

3.2 Instance data identification

W3C Direct Mapping

Each row becomes an instance (node) in the KG. It is identified by an IRI that combines the table name and the primary key value.

<People/ID=7>                   # single PK
<Order/customer=7;item=42>      # composite PK separated by ;

R2RML / our project

The IRI format itself is free. The standard simply provides a guide for "how names are constructed during automatic translation." We define readable forms such as bdc:Person_7 directly.

In our project, each row of a BDC Virtual Table generates an instance IRI in this pattern.

bdc:Person_7 a bdc:Person ;
    rdfs:label "Bob" .

💡 Always attach rdfs:label to instances. IRIs are identifiers, not human-readable names. SPARQL results returning only IRIs are difficult for an LLM to interpret.

3.3 Data attribute representation

W3C Direct Mapping

Each column of a table is translated into a literal property. The predicate name is automatically generated in the tableName#columnName form. For example, the fname column of the People table becomes <People#fname>.

<People/ID=7> <People#fname> "Bob" .
<People/ID=7> <People#ID>    7 .

NULL values do not produce triples.

R2RML / our project

Predicate names can be replaced with business vocabulary. fname is the column name, but the business term name is more appropriate, so we define bdc:name directly. Property definitions (domain, range, label) must be written separately in the ontology TTL.

# Defined in ontology TTL
bdc:name rdf:type owl:DatatypeProperty ;
    rdfs:domain bdc:Person ;
    rdfs:range  xsd:string ;
    rdfs:label  "Name" .

# Used in instance TTL
bdc:Person_7 bdc:name "Bob" .

3.4 Relationship representation

W3C Direct Mapping

A single FK column produces two triples.

<People/ID=7> <People#addressID>     18 .                    # the value itself (literal triple)
<People/ID=7> <People#ref-addressID> <Addresses/ID=18> .     # the reference (reference triple)
  • literal triple: preserves the original FK value.
  • reference triple: a graph edge pointing to another instance.

However, the predicate name is fixed in the #ref-colName form and cannot carry business meaning. Furthermore, Direct Mapping creates a reference triple only when an FK is declared.

R2RML / our project

R2RML does not require FK declarations. As long as the values exist, a relationship can be created.

If FKs cover 100% of relationships, Direct Mapping alone may be sufficient. In real SAP data, two reasons make this rarely the case.

First, once classes are split, the relationships between those classes must be defined explicitly. When MaterialType partitions Products into FinishedGood / SemiFinished / RawMaterial, the relationships among them (such as hasComponent) are determined by domain analysis, not by FKs.

Second, when a business relationship spans multiple tables (multi-hop), a single FK cannot connect them directly. This is why we precompute relationships with SQL Views to flatten them into 1-hop edges.

Ultimately, relationships are determined by people who know the domain, not by FKs.

There are three approaches to creating relationships:

Approach A: Assemble the IRI directly from a value (simple case)

Take the value of the FK column and construct an IRI directly. There is no need to look at the neighboring table. If addressID is 18, construct the IRI bdc:Address_18 and create the relationship triple.

bdc:Person_7 bdc:hasAddress bdc:Address_18 .

Approach B: Precompute the relationship with a SQL View (complex case)

For multi-hop or complex JOIN relationships, we precompute with a SQL View. The View returns (subj_id, obj_id) pairs, and Python reads them to build triples.

-- KGR_WC_PRODUCES_PRODUCT View result (example)
subj_id      | obj_id
-------------|----------
Z_ASM3       | MZ-FG-C900
Z_ASM3       | MZ-FG-C950

→ bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3 bdc:producesProduct bdc:FinishedGood_MZ-FG-C900

Our project's KGR_WC_LOCATEDAT_PLANT, KGR_WC_PRODUCES_PRODUCT, and others use this approach.

Approach C: Connect by shared column, even without FK (simple case)

Even without an FK constraint, if both tables share a column with the same meaning, Approach A applies directly. For example, the People table and the Addresses table are linked by addressID, but no FK is declared:

People table                       Addresses table
ID=7, addressID=18     →           ID=18, city=Seoul
      ↓ no FK declared
      Use addressID value 18 to build the IRI → bdc:Address_18

→ bdc:Person_7 bdc:hasAddress bdc:Address_18

The relationship works as long as the value exists. Our SQL Views also operate independently of whether FKs are declared.

In practice, all three are used. Simple FK relationships use Approach A, connections without FKs use Approach C, and complex multi-hop relationships use Approach B. The right choice depends on the situation, and all three approaches appeared in our project.

 

 Direct MappingR2RML / our project
FK declaration requiredYesNo (values are enough)
Relationship nameFixed as #ref-colNameFree (bdc:hasAddress, business vocabulary)
Defined byMachine (W3C spec)Human (domain expert)
Multi-hopNot supported (only direct FK)Supported via precomputed SQL Views, flattened to 1-hop

Combined example: how two tables become a graph

Original SQL tables

-- People table
ID  | fname | addressID
----+-------+----------
7   | Bob   | 18

-- Addresses table
ID  | city
----+------
18  | Seoul

Step 1: Direct Mapping automatic result

<People/ID=7>     rdf:type <People> .
<Addresses/ID=18> rdf:type <Addresses> .
<People/ID=7>     <People#fname>           "Bob" .
<People/ID=7>     <People#addressID>       18 .
<Addresses/ID=18> <Addresses#city>         "Seoul" .
<People/ID=7>     <People#ref-addressID>   <Addresses/ID=18> .

The data has been translated, but the predicates carry technical names (<People#fname>, <People#ref-addressID>).

Step 2: Layer an ontology to add business meaning

# ontology TTL
bdc:Person   rdf:type owl:Class ; rdfs:label "Person" .
bdc:Address  rdf:type owl:Class ; rdfs:label "Address" .
bdc:hasName    rdf:type owl:DatatypeProperty ;
               rdfs:domain bdc:Person ; rdfs:range xsd:string .
bdc:hasAddress rdf:type owl:ObjectProperty ;
               rdfs:domain bdc:Person ; rdfs:range bdc:Address .
bdc:inCity     rdf:type owl:DatatypeProperty ;
               rdfs:domain bdc:Address ; rdfs:range xsd:string .

# instance TTL (with business vocabulary)
<People/ID=7>     rdf:type bdc:Person ;
                  rdfs:label "Bob" ;
                  bdc:hasName "Bob" ;
                  bdc:hasAddress <Addresses/ID=18> .
<Addresses/ID=18> rdf:type bdc:Address ;
                  rdfs:label "Seoul" ;
                  bdc:inCity "Seoul" .

The standard's automatic mapping is the starting point; layering ontology definitions to add business meaning is the destination.

📖 Sources:

  • W3C, A Direct Mapping of Relational Data to RDF (Recommendation, 2012-09-27). https://www.w3.org/TR/rdb-direct-mapping/
  • W3C, R2RML: RDB to RDF Mapping Language (Recommendation, 2012-09-27). https://www.w3.org/TR/r2rml/

4. Three Domain Decisions We Made on Top of the Standards

The mappings defined by the W3C standards work well for simple relational databases. For data with a rich business domain inside, such as SAP BDC Data Products, a few domain decisions on top of the standard mappings are required to make the KG genuinely useful. We encountered three such points in our project.

Decision 1. Class Partition: one table, multiple classes

The default behavior of Direct Mapping is to map one table to one class. This is sufficient for simple RDBs, but in SAP's Product table, finished goods, semi-finished, and raw materials are mixed in the same table and distinguished only by the MaterialType column. Mapped as-is, all three would become a single bdc:Product.

→ Decision: assign different classes per row based on a discriminator column. Covered in detail in Part 2-2.

Decision 2. Multi-hop relationships: business relationships beyond direct FKs

Direct Mapping creates reference triples only from declared FKs. This has two implications.

(a) If an FK constraint is missing from the schema, no relationship triple is produced. The column value remains as a literal, and the two entities are not connected.

(b) A relationship requires a direct FK. SAP S/4HANA business relationships are often expressed through several intermediate tables. The relationship between WorkCenter and finished goods, for example, spans three routing tables, forming a 4-hop chain. This can be expressed with R2RML join conditions, but we chose a simpler approach.

→ Decision: precompute multi-hop chains with SQL Views and flatten them into 1-hop relationships. Covered in detail in Part 3 Section 3.

Decision 3. Junction tables: keep as node, or flatten

N:N relationships in SQL are typically expressed via junction tables (intermediate tables linking two tables). In our demo, PurchasingSourceList (PSL) is such a junction: a list of suppliers approved to provide each product.

When moving such a junction into a KG, there are two options.

Option 1. Keep as a node (Direct Mapping's default behavior): PSL becomes a separate node in the graph, with relationships connecting it to both sides.

→ The PSL node serves as a bridge between Supplier and Product. PSL can carry additional attributes such as plant, validity period, and priority.

Option 2. Flatten (direct relationship): drop the PSL node and connect Supplier directly to Product.

→ Cleaner, but the additional information PSL carries disappears. For example, if the same supplier-product pair has a different priority per plant, or if expired registrations must be distinguished from current ones, that information is lost. The fact that "Supplier A supplies Product X" remains, but "when, at which plant, with what priority" can no longer be answered by the KG.

Our decision: PSL is not a simple N:N connection.  It carries additional business information (plant, validity period, priority), so it is kept as a node. Two relationships are made explicit in the ontology.

bdc:fromSupplier a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain bdc:PurchasingSourceList ;
    rdfs:range  bdc:Supplier .

bdc:forProduct a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain bdc:PurchasingSourceList ;
    rdfs:range  bdc:Product .

→ PSL is placed in the graph as the class bdc:PurchasingSourceList, connected to both sides via bdc:fromSupplier and bdc:forProduct.

→ The decision criterion is whether the junction represents a simple connection or carries additional meaning. A junction that expresses only a simple connection should be flattened. The ontology result in Part 2-2, where PSL is classified as a bdc:StructuralNode (bridge node), reflects this decision.

Standard mapping vs our decisions: at a glance

 

Standard defaultOur domain decisionWhere
1 Table = 1 ClassClass Partition by discriminator columnPart 2-2
Reference triples only from direct FKsMulti-hop precomputed into 1-hop via SQL ViewsPart 3 Section 3
Junction table represented as an intermediate nodeMeaningful junctions kept as nodes; simple connections flattenedPart 2-2

These three decisions are the domain-level additions we layered when translating the SAP BDC domain into a KG. The standard mapping provides a baseline; the domain decisions add business meaning on top.

The actual application of these decisions begins in the next post (Part 2-2).

Summary

This post covered:

  • Core ontology concepts: Class/Instance (T-Box/A-Box), Domain & Range, Competency Questions, and LLM affinity all rooted in Ontology 101.
  • The seven-step process of Ontology 101: from determining the domain to creating instances. Iteration is the essence.
  • W3C RDB→RDF mapping standards: Direct Mapping (automatic) and R2RML (manual and flexible). Table → Class, Row → Instance, Column → Literal Property, FK → ObjectProperty.
  • A preview of domain decisions on top of the standards: Class Partition, multi-hop, junction handling, covered as concrete decisions in the next post.

What's Next

The background is now in place. From here on, we move into the actual decisions we made for SAP BDC data.

Part 2-2 – Designing the Ontology for SAP Data covers the domain decisions we made on top of these standards: which entities to place in the KG and which to leave in SQL, why we split a single Product table into three classes, what the final ontology structure looks like, and how we used AI to draft the ontology.

Stack: SAP BDC Data Products · HANA Cloud KGE · FastAPI · React · Claude (Anthropic / AI Core) Code: github.com/claudiopark86/sap-bdc-kge-agent-workshop

References

  • Ontology Development 101: A Guide to Creating Your First Ontology by Noy & McGuinness, Stanford 2001. https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf
  • A Direct Mapping of Relational Data to RDF, W3C Recommendation, 2012-09-27. https://www.w3.org/TR/rdb-direct-mapping/
  • R2RML: RDB to RDF Mapping Language, W3C Recommendation, 2012-09-27. https://www.w3.org/TR/r2rml/

entry.title: Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 2-2: Designing the Ontology for SAP Data
entry.content:
This blog is part of a blog series on building AI Agents with SAP BDC (Business Data Cloud) Data Products and SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine:

  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 1: Why Knowledge Graph for SAP Data
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 2-1: Ontology Standards and Concepts
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 2-2: Designing the Ontology for SAP Data 
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 3: Data Pipeline from BDC to HANA Cloud KGE
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 4: Building an Agent with SPARQL and SQL
  • Knowledge Graph Agent on SAP HANA Cloud Series – Part 5: Lessons Learned

Enterprise systems are vast, with thousands of tables, hundreds of business processes, and countless APIs, all interconnected in ways only domain experts fully grasp. For AI Agents to be genuinely useful in this environment, they need to understand not just what the data is, but how it all connects: which Product belongs to which Sales Order, which WorkCenter is located in which Plant, which Supplier serves which material. This understanding does not come from the data itself. It needs a dedicated semantic layer that makes business meaning explicit and machine-readable. Knowledge Graphs provide exactly that layer. SAP HANA Cloud now offers a native Knowledge Graph Engine to build it on top of SAP BDC Data Products. This series walks through the full journey: from why this layer matters, to how to design and build it, to how an AI Agent uses it to answer real business questions.

This post covers the ontology design decisions we made for real SAP BDC data: which entities to place in the KG versus leave in SQL, why we split a single Product table into three classes, what the final ontology structure looks like, and how we used AI to draft the Turtle file, including multilingual labels, comments, and synonyms.

💡 Reading guide. This post is designed to stand on its own. Section 0 below briefly covers the minimum KG modeling vocabulary required to follow along (the table/row/column/relationship-link mapping and why rdfs:label matters). Section 1 prepares the environment, and the substantive work begins in Section 2. If you want the deeper W3C standards background and the conceptual foundation, consult Part 2-1 – Ontology Standards and Concepts.

📁 Code reference. All scripts and project files mentioned in this post are available in the demo repository: github.com/claudiopark86/sap-bdc-kge-agent-workshop

File names such as 00_hana_setup.sql, 01_setup_synonyms.py, 02_verify_joins.py, dp_mapping.yaml, and 04_generate_ontology.py refer to files in that repository.

Table of contents:

  1. KG vocabulary essentials
  2. Preparing the work environment: BDC connection, users, synonyms
  3. Organizing your data as ontology material
  4. Deciding between KG and SQL
  5. Splitting one Data Product into multiple classes (Class Partition)
  6. Completing the ontology
  7. Drafting the ontology Turtle with AI, and writing labels

0. KG Vocabulary Essentials

Before moving into the work itself, here is the minimum KG modeling vocabulary required to follow this post. If you have read Part 2-1 in detail, this is a brief refresher; if you are seeing it for the first time, this much is sufficient to follow Sections 2–6.

0.1 Relational DB → KG mapping (at a glance)

The core mapping between a relational database and an RDF Knowledge Graph, as defined by the W3C:

Relational DBKnowledge GraphExample
TableClass (concept)WORK_CENTER table → bdc:WorkCenter class
RowInstance (actual entity)Row Z_ASM3 → bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3 instance
ColumnDatatypeProperty (attribute / literal property)WorkCenterName → bdc:workCenterName "Z_ASM3"
Relationship link (FK and similar)ObjectProperty (relationship)PlantID → bdc:locatedAt bdc:Plant_1010

⚠️ This table shows the basic mapping principles. In practice, the correspondence is rarely 1:1. A single table may be split into multiple classes (for example, splitting MaterialType into FinishedGood/SemiFinished/RawMaterial via Class Partition), or conversely, multiple tables may form a single class (for example, WorkCenter + WorkCenterCapacity → bdc:WorkCenter). These domain decisions are covered in Section 4 of this Part and in Part 2-1 Sections 3–4.

The key point is that relationship links are what make a graph a graph. More precisely: an FK (or a column shared between two tables) is a hint that "these two entities can be connected." Which business meaning to assign to that connection, and which name (ObjectProperty) to use, is a decision made by a domain expert. Direct Mapping looks at an FK and automatically produces a reference triple of the form #ref-colName; in R2RML, a person explicitly defines a business term such as bdc:locatedAt. The details are covered in Part 2-1 Section 3.

📖 Standard basis: The mapping above is grounded in the W3C Direct Mapping and R2RML recommendations (2012). The full standard definitions and the standard's limitations are covered in Part 2-1 Sections 3–4.

0.2 rdfs:label is the minimum requirement for a KG

Once instances are loaded into a KG, SPARQL results return URIs:

bdc:Supplier_1000015
bdc:Product_MZ-FG-C900
bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3

An LLM given only URIs has a hard time interpreting "what this is." Attach rdfs:label to every instance, class, and property, and the SPARQL results return human-readable names directly.

wcLabel       | productLabel
Z_ASM3        | C-Series Road Bike 900
Z_ASM3        | C-Series Road Bike 950

Without labels, the KG functions, but the Agent cannot interpret the results. Labels are the minimum requirement that makes a KG into "a graph an LLM can read."

Not only labels but comments, and not only on instances but on classes and relationships too

The instance example above illustrates "why a label is needed." In an actual ontology, one more property is required, and the places where label and comment are attached extend further.

  • rdfs:comment: where label provides "a short name," comment is a natural-language description. A one-line answer to "what is a WorkCenter?"
  • Attached to classes and relationships (ObjectProperty) as well: not only on instances but on the class definitions themselves (T-Box). Tools such as Protégé and Metaphactory display labels instead of URIs, and when the Part 4 Agent loads ontology metadata into its LLM context, comments serve as the explanation of "what role this class plays."
bdc:WorkCenter a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label   "WorkCenter"@en, "작업장"@ko ;
    rdfs:comment "Production work center. Located in a Plant, produces Finished Goods."@en,
                 "생산 작업장. 특정 Plant 에 위치하며 FinishedGood 을 생산한다."@ko .

bdc:producesProduct a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:label   "produces product"@en, "제품 생산"@ko ;
    rdfs:comment "The FinishedGood that a WorkCenter produces."@en ;
    rdfs:domain  bdc:WorkCenter ;
    rdfs:range   bdc:FinishedGood .

 Standard basis: OWL 2 Quick Reference §2.7 defines rdfs:label and rdfs:comment as built-in annotation properties. These are "standard annotations that can be attached to any class, property, or instance." Major ontologies such as FOAF, schema.org, and Dublin Core commonly attach them to T-Box definitions.

The practical convention differs slightly depending on where they are attached:

Wherelabelcomment
Class · relationship (ObjectProperty) (T-Box)Nearly always — business name, verb phraseNearly always — concept description, LLM context
Instance (A-Box)Nearly always — identifier or name ("Z_ASM3")Rarely — typically replaced by a dedicated datatype property

Comments on the A-Box are rare because facts about an instance are more useful as discrete datatype properties than as free-form prose. They are easier to validate and query. Rather than the free-form "Z_ASM3 is the bicycle final-assembly line," the same information is split into bdc:workCenterTypeCode "A" or similar.

Internationalization (i18n): language tags in a single property

rdfs:label and rdfs:comment accept RDF's language-tagged literals directly. Multiple language values can sit on the same property with @en / @ko and other language tags (as in the Turtle example above). With this in place, the Part 4 Agent retrieves the user's language with a single line  FILTER(LANG(?label) = "ko")  in SPARQL. Multilingual support is delivered through the ontology alone, with no backend code changes.

When users call the same entity by different names: skos:altLabel

One more situation comes up: when users refer to the same entity by different names. Calling a "공급사" a "벤더," or using "Vendor" instead of "Supplier." rdfs:label is a vocabulary for assigning one preferred name per entity, so packing synonyms into it is awkward. This is where the W3C SKOS (Simple Knowledge Organization System) standard's skos:altLabel fills the gap.

bdc:Supplier  a  owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label     "Supplier"@en, "공급사"@ko ;
    skos:altLabel  "Vendor"@en, "벤더"@ko, "거래처"@ko .

When the Agent encounters an expression such as "vendor USSU-VSF01..." in a user question, the skos:altLabel in the ontology links it to bdc:Supplier. If rdfs:label is the "official name," skos:altLabel holds "the other recognized names." The SAP domain mixes ABAP abbreviations (EKKO, KNA1) with business vocabulary (PurchaseOrder, Customer), so this pattern fits especially well.

How labels, comments, and altLabels are applied in bulk is covered in Section 6. Rather than typing each one by hand for dozens of classes, an LLM given the YAML and domain context fills them in consistently.

1. Preparing the Work Environment: BDC Connection, Users, Synonyms

With the vocabulary established, we can now prepare the implementation environment. Before substantive ontology work begins, the environment must be ready. Three pieces are required:

  1. Source: Install an SAP BDC Data Product into HANA Cloud to obtain the Virtual Table and CSN metadata.
  2. User & Permissions: A working user with catalog read, Virtual Table SELECT, and ownership of the working schema.
  3. Synonym: A mapping that groups long Virtual Table names into short ones to make subsequent SQL queries and KG generation work easier.

ℹ️ About the .env file

The .env referenced throughout this post is a local-development environment variable file. It holds sensitive values, including HANA Cloud connection details (HANA_ADDRESS, HANA_PORT, HANA_USER, HANA_PASSWORD), SAP AI Core keys, AWS S3 keys, which the LLM references while writing code. It is required only for local testing; for production deployments, use a secret management service such as Kyma Secret.

All Vibe Coding prompts in this post assume .env is already configured.

ℹ️ About utils.py / get_hana_connection()

The utils.py referenced in the Vibe Coding prompts is a helper module that centralizes connections to external systems such as HANA Cloud, AI Core, S3. get_hana_connection() is the function that builds a HANA Cloud connection: it reads the .env connection details and returns an hdbcli connection object.

Because the LLM can call this single function instead of rewriting connection code each time, the resulting code is shorter and the only thing that needs to change when the environment shifts is .env. Copy our demo's utils.py into your project, or ask the LLM directly: "Create a utils.py with a get_hana_connection() function that reads .env and returns a HANA connection."

Source: connecting a BDC Data Product to HANA Cloud

📌 This section covers the hands-on setup that Part 1 Section6 introduced. 

 References. This series does not cover the detailed procedure from installing BDC Data Products into HANA Cloud and getting CSNs of them from SAP HANA Cloud Central as Part 1 already explained them. Please, check out the Part 1 and the the official SAP guides.

  • Tutorial: HANA Cloud Data Products Consumption
  • Official documentation: Data Product Support in SAP HANA Cloud

Here, you will find the steps: after download the CSN JSON files, where to put them and how to set up the HANA Cloud environment before ontology work begins.

Ontology design begins the moment an SAP BDC Data Product is shared into HANA Cloud. Select a Data Product in the BDC catalog and install it into HANA Cloud: a Virtual Table is created, and at the same time the CSN metadata (column names, types, FK associations, and so on) becomes available. With no separate script for extracting metadata, the raw material for ontology design is in place after a few clicks.

This metadata is the starting point for ontology design. When you write an ontology, you need to know precisely which entities, columns, and relationships exist, and which Competency Questions (business requirements) the ontology must address. The metadata addresses the first half.

📁 CSN metadata is gathered in the docs/dp_specs/ folder.

When you install each Data Product from BDC into HANA Cloud, you can download the CSN JSON file alongside it (from the BDC Catalog Data Product detail view, or the HANA Cloud Central Data Product tab). Place the downloaded files in your project's docs/dp_specs/ folder, and the LLM can reference them in bulk during the later Vibe Coding stages as docs/dp_specs/*_CSN.json.

docs/dp_specs/
  ├── WorkCenter_CSN.json
  ├── Product_CSN.json
  ├── Supplier_CSN.json
  ├── Customer_CSN.json
  └── ...

This folder becomes the shared input for Section 2 (CSN_SUMMARY authoring), Section 3 (KG / SQL classification), and Section 4 (Class Partition decisions).

For teams working directly with S/4HANA, as covered in Part 1 Section 1, CDS View definitions can serve as the same metadata raw material.

User & Permissions: a working user with catalog read access

To query installed Virtual Tables and use them for ontology work, a user with appropriate permissions is required. Rather than performing every operation as DBADMIN, it is cleaner to create a dedicated working user (for example, BDC_DP) and grant the following:

  • Read access to metadata views such as SYS.VIRTUAL_TABLES and PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS: for Virtual Table and column metadata exploration
  • SELECT on each BDC DP schema: for Virtual Table queries
  • Ownership of a working schema (for example, BDC_DP😞 where synonyms and KG helper Views will be created

💡 Our demo's 00_hana_setup.sql performs the same setup in one shot. It bundles user creation, BDC DP schema SELECT grants, system catalog read access, and working-schema creation into a single SQL script.

Synonym: shortening long Virtual Table names

This step is genuinely needed only after Section 3, once you have decided "which Data Products and which tables within them to place in the KG." It is sufficient to create synonyms only for the chosen tables. Still, because the flow "BDC connection → user setup → make SQL easier to write" reads naturally as one block, and because subsequent work flows more smoothly afterward, we cover it here in advance. Whether to define synonyms only for selected tables or for every Virtual Table brought from BDC is a matter of working style.

Immediately after installation, two names are auto-generated. A separate schema is created per Data Product, and the Virtual Table sits inside it:

DP schema name:
  _SAP_DATAPRODUCT_sap_s4com_dataProduct_WorkCenter_v1_<DP_GUID>

Virtual Table inside it:
  _SAP_DATAPRODUCT_<VT_GUID>_workcenter.WorkCenter

The schema itself is long, multiple DPs produce many schemas, and the table names inside each schema are long as well. Writing these out in every SQL statement is inefficient. Consolidating them into short synonyms in one place is the first step before substantive work begins.

CREATE SYNONYM BDC_DP.WORK_CENTER FOR
  "_SAP_DATAPRODUCT_<VT_GUID>_workcenter"."WorkCenter";

With this in place, subsequent SQL can use the short name BDC_DP.WORK_CENTER.

Vibe Coding: querying the Virtual Table list

📥Input

  • .env: HANA Cloud connection details (HANA_ADDRESS, HANA_PORT, HANA_USER, HANA_PASSWORD)

📤Output

  • virtual_tables.csv: full list of available BDC Virtual Tables (SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME)
  • Used in: Section 1 synonym creation, Section 2 CSN_SUMMARY authoring

This step is delegated to the LLM. The human reviewer's role is limited to confirming that the resulting CSV is produced correctly.

Make get_hana_connection() in utils.py read the HANA Cloud connection details
(HANA_ADDRESS, HANA_PORT, HANA_USER, HANA_PASSWORD) from .env and connect to
HANA Cloud.

Then use this function to query SCHEMA_NAME and TABLE_NAME from the
SYS.VIRTUAL_TABLES view, and save the result as virtual_tables.csv.

Use docs/dp_specs/virtual_tables_template.csv as the column structure reference (SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME — synonym columns will be added in the next step)

Vibe Coding: synonym creation

📥Input

  • virtual_tables.csv: the VT list from the previous step (SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME)

📤Output

  • ingestion/01_setup_synonyms.py: synonym creation script
  • Short-name synonyms created in HANA's BDC_DP schema (long SAP table names → short names)
  • virtual_tables.csv updated: enriched CSV with synonym_schema and synonym_name columns added
  • Used in: Section 2 CSN_SUMMARY authoring, Section 2 JOIN verification, the full Part 3 pipeline

The LLM examines the VT list, proposes short synonym names, and generates the code for creating them. The human reviewer confirms that there are no collisions (no existing synonym with the same name) and that the short names match the domain vocabulary, then runs the script in the local development environment. Once the names are recorded in the CSV, subsequent stages can carry the VT ↔ synonym mapping in a single file.

For each VT in virtual_tables.csv, propose a short synonym name (for example,
WORK_CENTER, SALES_ORDER_ITEM) and write the code that creates each synonym
under my working schema (BDC_DP) into ingestion/01_setup_synonyms.py.
Run it immediately after writing, and update virtual_tables.csv by adding
synonym_schema and synonym_name columns.
The final CSV should match the 4-column structure in docs/dp_specs/virtual_tables_template.csv (SCHEMA_NAME, TABLE_NAME, synonym_schema, synonym_name).

Section 1 deliverables

StepDeliverableRole
BDC → HANA install (manual)docs/dp_specs/*_CSN.jsonInput for Section 2 CSN_SUMMARY
User & Permissions (manual SQL)Working user + working schemaContext for all subsequent SQL / synonym creation
Query VT list (Vibe Coding)virtual_tables.csvInput for synonym creation and CSN_SUMMARY VT matching
Create synonyms (Vibe Coding)01_setup_synonyms.py + HANA synonyms + enriched virtual_tables.csvShort names for SQL queries and later KG generation steps, the working environment for Sections 2–6

Section 1 ends here as environment preparation. The substantive question, "what to design on top of this data" (KG vs SQL classification, classes, relationships), begins at Section 2 with the CSN_SUMMARY and accumulates in dp_mapping.yaml.

2. Organizing Your Data as Ontology Material

With the environment ready, the actual ontology work begins. Authoring the CSN_SUMMARY, drafting dp_mapping.yaml, and verifying JOIN candidates for relationships fall within the scope of Section 2.

🎯 What you will build starting in Section 2: dp_mapping.yaml (the single source of truth for ontology work)

If Section 1 was environment setup, Section 2 starts the design decisions. The file in which those decisions accumulate is dp_mapping.yaml, and it is the single source of truth that collects every decision made across Sections 2–6. "Which DPs go into the KG, which entities become which classes, how to partition". All of these decisions converge in one file. The blank template is in ingestion/dp_mapping.template.yaml, and the final form follows a five-region structure:

# ingestion/dp_mapping.template.yaml (excerpt, summarized)
synonym_schema: &synonym_schema "BDC_DP"     # the synonym schema created in Section 1

# ─── Master Data: core node DPs that serve as endpoints of relationships (the start/end of CQs) ───
master_data:
  - name: <DP. e.g., WorkCenter>
    kg_class: <bdc:ClassName>
    description: "<one-line DP description>"
    virtual_table_schema: "_SAP_DATAPRODUCT_..._<DP>_v1_<DP_GUID>"
    entities:
      - entity: <entity name>
        synonym_schema: *synonym_schema
        synonym_name: "<SHORT_NAME>"
        virtual_table_name: "_SAP_DATAPRODUCT_<VT_GUID>_<dp>.<Entity>"
    # partition [Optional]: added for DPs that require Class Partition (Section 4 decision)
    partition:
      discriminator: <column>
      parent_class: <kg_class above>
      subclasses:
        - value: <column value>
          kg_class: <bdc:SubClass>

# ─── Structural Data [Optional]: multi-hop bridge / junction roles ───
# If master_data alone connects all relationships, this category can stay empty.
structural_data:
  - name: <DP>
    role: <multi-hop-intermediate | bridge-with-attributes>
    ...

# ─── Transaction Data: not loaded into the KG, used as SQL aggregation targets ───
transaction_data:
  - name: <DP>
    aggregation_columns: [<col1>, <col2>]
    ...

# ─── Relationships: ObjectProperty definitions + KGR View mapping (Section 5 decision) ───
relationships:
  - name: <relationship name, e.g., producesProduct>
    domain: <bdc:Class>
    range:  <bdc:Class>
    source:
      type: kgr_view
      view_name: <KGR_VIEW_NAME>
      sql_file: <path, e.g., ingestion/kgr_views/kgr_*.sql>

Because this YAML collects every decision in Sections 2–6, it appears repeatedly in the rest of this post. Following "where in the YAML each step's decision is recorded" gives a natural view of how the ontology comes together. For the full template and field descriptions, consult ingestion/dp_mapping.template.yaml directly.

One clarification: the YAML does not hold everything. The Turtle definitions of classes and properties themselves (including label and comment) are emitted in Section 6 as a separate ontology.ttl, and the SQL bodies of the KGR Views live in separate .sql files (the YAML's sql_file: points to the path). The YAML captures "which DP is classified where + where it comes from + how it is partitioned + which relationship is realized by which View." The semantics layered on top belong to the Turtle.

Organizing CSN metadata for ontology design

The design process starts with organizing the CSN (Core Schema Notation) metadata. The CSN specification for each BDC Data Product is available as JSON, but it contains every field and annotation. The full payload is too verbose to feed directly into ontology design.

So for each DP, the following information is distilled into a CSN_SUMMARY document:

  • Main entity name and ABAP source name
  • Key columns (PK + business key)
  • Relationship links (FK and similar): which column connects to which entity in which DP
  • Amount / quantity columns: fields that may become SQL aggregation targets
  • KG / SQL classification candidate: master data or transaction

This task can also be delegated to the LLM. Hand it the CSN JSON files and the blank template together and ask: "Organize each DP's main entity, keys, FKs, and amount columns into a table." A person reads the result and decides "this DP goes into the KG or SQL; this FK is a relationship candidate," and those decisions are persisted in the next step's dp_mapping.yaml.

📌 CSN_SUMMARY and dp_mapping.yaml have separate roles. CSN_SUMMARY is responsible for "a human-readable compression of raw CSN." The results of decisions, such as "DP category classification / multi-hop relationships / Class Partition / VT · synonym mapping" — belong in the next step's dp_mapping.yaml. Putting everything in a single document blurs the line between "compressed reference" and "decision," and any decision change requires editing two places.

Vibe Coding: authoring CSN_SUMMARY

📥Input

  • docs/dp_specs/*_CSN.json: per-DP CSN specs (downloaded alongside the BDC Data Product)
  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY_TEMPLATE.md: blank template (in the workshop materials, or the demo repository)
  • virtual_tables.csv (enriched): the Section 1 VT list + synonym information

📤Output

  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md: per-DP compressed reference filled in with domain data
    • Per DP: main entity / key columns / relationship links / amount · quantity columns / tentative KG · SQL classification
  • Used in: direct input for the Section 2 dp_mapping.yaml draft

This is the most important step in this post. The LLM reads all CSN JSON files and fills in the template. The domain expert's task is to review whether the classification and relationship candidates fit the domain.

Copy docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY_TEMPLATE.md to docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md
and fill in every <...> placeholder using these input sources:
- dp_specs/*_CSN.json: per-DP main entity, key columns, FKs, amount/quantity columns
- virtual_tables.csv (with synonym info): match each DP to its available VT

For each DP, fill in every section of the TEMPLATE:
- Main entity / ABAP source name / column count
- Key columns table
- Relationship links table (FKs and similar candidate connections to other DPs, raw)
- Amount/quantity columns table
- Tentative KG / SQL classification + one-line reason

⚠️ Follow the TEMPLATE's format (sections / table columns) exactly. Adjust the domain
   vocabulary to fit your data.
⚠️ Do NOT include comprehensive decisions such as "DP category / multi-hop relationships /
   Class Partition" here. Those belong in the next step's dp_mapping.yaml.

💡 Our demo's docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md is the result of this step. From it, a person can see at a glance which DPs are master data, which are transactions, and which relationships are feasible.

Moving CSN_SUMMARY into a dp_mapping.yaml draft

If CSN_SUMMARY was the "per-DP compressed reference" of what each DP looks like, dp_mapping.yaml is the SSoT in which comprehensive decisions are persisted based on it. Decisions intentionally left out of the CSN_SUMMARY, such as "DP category classification / relationship candidates / VT · synonym mapping", collect here. The same information is then ① classified by DP-level category (master / structural / transaction), ② grouped with each DP's VT / synonym information, and ③ given placeholders for downstream decisions (kg_class, multi-hop relationship definitions, and so on).

📌 CSN_SUMMARY → YAML mapping. The following moves from CSN_SUMMARY into the YAML:

  • Per-DP relationship links (FKs and similar) → the basis for tentative master_data placement (after Section 2 JOIN verification, some shift to structural_data)
  • Per-DP tentative KG / SQL classification → tentative category values in the YAML (in Section 3, some move to transaction_data)
  • VT / synonym mapping from the Section 1 enriched CSV → virtual_table_schema, virtual_table_name, synonym_name in the YAML
  • Amount / quantity columns → (not persisted in the YAML; the Part 4 SQL tool references CSN_SUMMARY directly)

Vibe Coding: drafting dp_mapping.yaml

📥Input

  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md: the per-DP compressed reference from Section 2
  • virtual_tables.csv (enriched): the Section 1 VT list + synonym information
  • ingestion/dp_mapping.template.yaml: blank template (in the workshop materials, or the demo repository)

📤Output

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml draft: all DPs tentatively placed in the master_data category, with each DP's virtual_table_schema, entity list, and synonym_name filled in
  • Used in: Section 2 JOIN verification, Section 3 KG vs SQL classification, Section 4 Class Partition, Section 5 final Ontology

The LLM reads the CSN_SUMMARY and the enriched CSV and fills in the YAML skeleton. A person confirms only that the tentative category placement is correct and that the synonym names are persisted accurately.

Follow the structure of ingestion/dp_mapping.template.yaml exactly and produce
an ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml draft.

Input sources:
- docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md : per-DP main entity, keys, relationship links,
  amounts/quantities, tentative KG/SQL classification
- virtual_tables.csv (enriched) : VT list + synonym_schema, synonym_name

What to fill in:
- Place every DP tentatively in master_data (I will reclassify in later steps)
- Leave structural_data and transaction_data with their keys present and empty lists
- Leave relationships with its key and an empty list (I will fill in relationship
  definitions during ontology design)
- For each DP, fill in name / description / virtual_table_schema / entities[]
  - entities[*]: entity, synonym_schema, synonym_name, virtual_table_name
- Fill in kg_class from the CSN_SUMMARY tentative classification + domain common sense
  (for example, WorkCenter DP → bdc:WorkCenter)
- Fill in metadata such as ord_id and priority from CSN_SUMMARY or the BDC catalog

⚠️ Follow the TEMPLATE's key names, indentation, and category order exactly.
⚠️ Do NOT add a partition (Class Partition) block in this draft — I will add it to
   the relevant DPs during the Class Partition decision step.
⚠️ Do NOT add items inside relationships in this draft — I will fill them in during
   ontology design. Leave the key present and the list empty.
⚠️ Do NOT add datatype_properties for any DP in this draft — I will organize them
   during ontology design once the class hierarchy is finalized.

Finally, do not just author the YAML — verify it directly and report the result:
- Confirm that yaml.safe_load() parses successfully
- Confirm that every DP is placed in exactly one of the three categories
  (compare against the DP list in CSN_SUMMARY; nothing missing)
- Confirm that each DP's synonym_name matches the value in virtual_tables.csv
  (enriched)
- Confirm that entities[*].virtual_table_name is not empty
If verification is not clean, briefly explain what went wrong, fix the YAML, and
verify again.
This is the input for every subsequent step, so a defect here surfaces only much
later — validate it thoroughly at this stage.

dp_mapping.yaml evolves step by step

The draft above is not completed in one pass. Immediately after setup, every DP is placed in master_data (tentative classification), and the decisions made in the later half of Section 2 through Section 5 refine the YAML classification.

⚠️The YAML classifies at the DP level. "This DP leans toward a relationship-bridging role" is the coarse classification the YAML captures. The finer entity-level decisions, such as "this entity is a KG node, this entity is a bridge, this entity is ignored" — are addressed in detail in Section 4 (Class Partition) and Part 3 (KGR Views).

Point in timeYAML changeResult of which decision
Immediately after draftAll 9 DPs in master_data (tentative), relationships empty list"Everything brought in for now"
After Section 2 JOIN verificationProductionRouting, PurchasingSourceList → structural_data (optional category)Discovered as bridge / junction roles
Section 3 KG vs SQLSalesOrder, PurchaseOrder, BillingDocument, ProdOrderConfirmation → transaction_dataDecision: "not in the KG, SQL only"
Section 4 Class Partitionpartition block added to the Product DP (FERT/HALB/ROH → FinishedGood/SemiFinished/RawMaterial)"One DP, three classes"
Section 5 final Ontologyrelationships filled with ObjectProperty definitions + KGR View mappingsSemantic relationships between classes confirmed
Section 6 Turtle authoringYAML stops changing. ontology.ttl is generated from YAML + separate label/commentReady for loading
