Keep this table in mind as you read Sections 2 (latter half) through 5, and "where in the YAML each decision is persisted" becomes apparent. At the end of each subsection, we briefly summarize the "YAML changes resulting from this step."

CSN relationship links (FKs and similar) are a starting point; bind business relationships on top and verify with actual JOINs

CSN metadata indicates "this column is the relationship link (FK) of that table" . However, that is only a starting point. The ontology does not end there. Domain analysis adds business relationships not present in CSN (for example, "which Products a WorkCenter produces,"  a relationship the domain requires but that is not directly expressed as a standard relationship link), and relationships that span different Data Products (for example, locatedAt between WorkCenter in the WorkCenter DP and Plant in the Plant DP) must be bound by a person. CSN reports only relationships within a single DP; cross-DP relationships are filled in with domain knowledge. Whether a relationship comes from CSN, spans DPs, or is a business relationship, whether the JOIN actually works on real data must be verified separately.

This post follows R2RML principles when defining and verifying relationships. Regardless of whether the relationship is declared in CSN, as long as the values exist and a domain expert designates it a relationship, it becomes one. For the detailed R2RML standard, consult Part 2-1 Section 3.

It is common for metadata to declare a relationship while the data is missing, or for data types to mismatch subtly, or for one side's column to have so many NULLs that the relationship is effectively broken. Missing this during ontology authoring leads to "the relationship was declared, yet SPARQL returns zero rows" after loading.

The verification approach: execute the actual JOINs for both CSN's FK candidates and the business relationship candidates we added, and check the result row counts. If the result is 0 or far fewer than expected, that relationship is dropped from the ontology, or the data source must be revisited.

💡Our demo's ingestion/02_verify_joins.py performs this work. It collects the JOIN candidates (as SQL JOIN statements) within the script, executes them one by one, and prints the matching row counts. This script revealed that every row of ProductionRoutingHeader.WorkCenterInternalID in our demo data was '00000000', breaking the routing connection (the full case is in Part 5 Challenge 2), and triggered a redesign of the multi-hop path in the ontology.

This verification is repeated every time a new relationship is added to the ontology. The verification work becomes the foundation for the data pipeline in the next post (Part 3). Only verified relationships are persisted as ObjectProperties in the ontology, and the resulting KG actually works.

📌 This verification report is the basis for the decisions in Sections 3–5. Section 3's KG vs SQL classification, Section 4's Class Partition, and Section 5's final ontology structure, all are decisions made after confirming "which relationships are alive in the real data." Without verification, guessing at the ontology leads to SPARQL returning zero rows after loading.

📒 YAML changes from this step + one domain decision.

JOIN verification revealed two DPs that play "relationship / edge roles." ProductionRouting (a multi-hop intermediate between WorkCenter and Product) and PurchasingSourceList (a Supplier ↔ Product bridge). Both move from master_data to structural_data. One more decision is needed: how to handle multi-hop bridges in the KG.

Two paths when encountering a multi-hop bridge:

Option How the bridge is handledProsCons
(a) Direct connect (flatten)The bridge entity is not persisted in the KG; only the endpoints (A → C) are connected as a 1-hop relationshipThe KG stays clean, multi-hop does not pollute visualization, SPARQL is simpleInformation carried by the bridge itself is invisible in the KG
(b) Persist the bridge as a nodeThe bridge entity is loaded into the KG as a separate class (A → B → C)The bridge's attributes (plant, period, priority) become explorable via SPARQLNode count increases, intermediate nodes appear in visualizations

We chose different answers for the two DPs.

  • ProductionRouting → (a) flatten. The routing header / operations themselves carry no attributes worth querying via SPARQL beyond the fact that "this WorkCenter produces this Product." A SQL View that compresses 4-hop into 1-hop (covered in Part 3) is created, and no nodes are loaded into the KG.
  • PurchasingSourceList → (b) persist as a node. PSL carries information of its own, such as plant, validity period, and priority beyond the basic "this supplier provides this material." Answering questions such as "show only priority-1 sources" requires PSL to be a node.

Both DPs go into the structural_data category because both serve "a supporting role connecting the core nodes in master_data." Within that category, whether a node is persisted in the KG (b) depends on whether the domain wants to ask further questions of that bridge. The fate of each is depicted concretely in Section 5 (final Ontology).

structural_data is an optional category that may be left empty depending on the scenario. If the core nodes in master_data alone connect every relationship, there is no need for it. We separated it as its own category because multi-hop is in play and a junction with its own attributes (PSL) is present.

Vibe Coding: the JOIN verification script

📥Input

  • ingestion/02_verify_joins.py (workshop materials / demo repository): the infrastructure code + JOINS / SAMPLE_QUERIES structure as a reference. Hand the LLM the file and say "following the structure and output format, fill in the JOINS for our domain only."
  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml: synonym mapping (BDC_DP.* short names)
  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md: relationship candidates + KG / SQL classification
  • Competency Questions (CQs): the business questions the KG must answer

📤Output

  • ingestion/02_verify_joins.py: a verification script populated with your domain's JOIN candidates
  • Verification report: per-candidate row count and match rate (execution stdout / log)
  • Used in: the basis for Section 3 KG vs SQL classification, Section 4 Class Partition, and Section 5 final ontology decisions

The verification script mixes work requiring domain knowledge ("is this relationship business-meaningful") with mechanical repetition ("count the rows of this JOIN"). Delegating the latter to the LLM lets a person focus exclusively on the former. The prompt should contain four elements:

  1. Specify the metadata source. Tell the LLM where to find candidates. Point it at the SYS.VIRTUAL_TABLES / PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS metadata views, and hand it dp_mapping.yaml (the VT → synonym mapping) so it writes SQL with short names (BDC_DP.WORK_CENTER). Without the YAML, the LLM guesses long VT names and fails.
  2. Provide the Competency Questions (CQs). Hand over "the questions the KG must answer" so the LLM can narrow the candidates by "which relationship resolves this question" (for example, "who is affected by a WorkCenter shutdown?" → the WorkCenter ↔ Product ↔ SalesOrder ↔ Customer chain becomes the verification target).
  3. Concrete verification criteria. Replace vague language such as "works well" with numeric thresholds: row counts, NULL ratios, minimum matching thresholds.
  4. Human-reviewable output format. Tabular format + sample rows. "What you can see with your eyes" becomes the input for the domain decision.
Good prompt:

"Following the structure and output format of ingestion/02_verify_joins.py
exactly, rewrite ingestion/02_verify_joins.py for our domain. Replace only
the JOINS and SAMPLE_QUERIES lists; do NOT modify the infrastructure
functions (run_join_checks, run_sample_queries, main).

[Input materials / attach together]
- 02_verify_joins.py (existing) : structure and output format reference
- dp_mapping.yaml                : VT → synonym mapping (BDC_DP.* short names)
- (if needed) CSN_SUMMARY.md     : relationship candidates (per-DP reference)

Competency Questions the KG must answer:
- CQ1: When a specific WorkCenter goes down, which customers' revenue is affected?
- CQ2: Which Product is produced at which Plant?
- CQ3: Which Supplier supplies which Product?
... (as many as needed)

First organize which relationship chains are needed to answer these CQs, and
on top of those chains, populate the JOINS list with three kinds of
candidates:

(a) Direct relationship links within one DP : identify candidates by column
    name patterns (for example, '*ID', '*Code')
(b) Relationships across DPs                 : the same column name or the
    same domain values appearing in multiple DPs
(c) Multi-hop business relationships         : chains added through domain
    analysis (I will provide the JOIN SQL separately)

Write SQL with the short synonym names in dp_mapping.yaml (BDC_DP.WORK_CENTER
and similar). Place each entry in the same tuple form as the existing file
(label, key columns description, SQL).

Additionally, populate SAMPLE_QUERIES with 3–5 spot-check queries that a person
can verify by eye (for example, 'top 10 WorkCenters', 'sample 5 rows joined
via a specific relationship').

Finally, do not just author the script — execute it and show me the results.
- If every JOIN matches with ✅, you are done.
- If any item returns ⚠️ 0 rows or ⛔ an error, briefly hypothesize the cause
  (wrong column name? missing data? a different path needed?) and propose a
  fix. Once I approve, modify JOINS and re-run. Repeat until the result is
  clean.

[After verification passes, persist candidates to the YAML]
Once verification is clean, persist the surviving JOINs into the relationships
section of dp_mapping.yaml as 'free-form candidates.' Do not yet populate the
formal ObjectProperty form (name/domain/range/source) — I will organize that
during ontology design.

Example (one item each, free-form description only):
relationships:
  - description: "WorkCenter → ProductionRoutingOpSubord (key: WorkCenterInternalID+TypeCode, matched: 8,432 rows)"
  - description: "Product → ProductPlant via Material (matched: 3,197 rows)"
  - ...

⚠️ Do NOT touch master_data / structural_data / transaction_data / partition /
   datatype_properties.
⚠️ After modifying the YAML, confirm yaml.safe_load() parses successfully."

→ Once the LLM organizes "which relationship chains are needed to answer the CQs" and writes the verification script, a person reads the results and decides "keep this relationship, drop that one." Our demo's 02_verify_joins.py was built exactly this way, so handing that file as a reference lets the LLM fill in JOINs for your own domain in the same structure and format.

Bad prompt:

"Verify that the FKs work properly."
→ "Properly" has no criterion. The LLM looks at the metadata only and says "FK
   is declared, so OK."
→ Without the CQs the KG must answer, no way to distinguish important
   relationships.
→ Without the synonym list, the LLM guesses long VT names or writes generic SQL.
→ Cross-DP and multi-hop relationships are out of sight entirely.

Roles:

  • LLM: drafting dp_mapping.yaml + step-by-step updates (moving DPs between master_data / structural_data / transaction_data based on verification results, filling in kg_class), writing catalog query SQL, deriving relationship chains from the CQs, writing JOIN execution scripts, organizing result tables, flagging clearly broken relationships (zero matches)
  • Domain expert: ① defining CQs (the list of business questions the KG must answer), ② reviewing whether the LLM's proposed YAML classification and naming match the domain vocabulary, ③ deciding which relationship candidates to add to the verification target (especially cross-DP and multi-hop), ④ judging whether to keep or drop relationships with low match rates, ⑤ analyzing whether broken relationships are due to missing data or whether they can be routed differently

Section 2 deliverables:

Step Deliverable RoleStep DeliverableRole
CSN_SUMMARY authoringdocs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.mdPer-DP main entity / keys / relationship links (FKs and similar) / amounts · quantities (compressed per DP)
dp_mapping.yaml draftingestion/dp_mapping.yamlSSoT draft with category classification, VT / synonym mapping, and kg_class placeholders filled in
JOIN verification scriptingestion/02_verify_joins.py + verification reportMatch rate and NULL ratio per relationship candidate, the basis for decisions in Sections 3–5

3. Deciding Between KG and SQL

After working through Section 2, the categorization in dp_mapping.yaml is refined once. Core node DPs are in master_data, DPs that emerged as bridges are in structural_data, and the transaction_data slot is still empty.

This section makes two decisions:

  1. Section 3.1: transactional data (numeric aggregation targets) go to SQL, moving to transaction_data
  2. Section 3.2: master data orphaned by those moves (entities that became "islands" in the KG) also go to SQL

After these two decisions, only "master data whose structural relationships must be traversed" remains in the KG.

Entities whose structural relationships must be traversed → KG. Data that must be aggregated numerically → SQL. Entities that are not connected to other nodes within the KG → SQL.

Our final classification (instance counts below are SAP demo data examples):

KG (master data, structural relationships):
├── WorkCenter   , work center (129 examples)
├── Product      , finished / semi-finished / raw material (2,593 examples)
├── Supplier     , supplier (153 examples)
├── Plant        , plant (12 examples)
└── PurchasingSourceList  ← Supplier ↔ Product bridge node

SQL (transactional, numeric):
├── SalesOrder / SalesOrderItem
├── PurchaseOrder / PurchaseOrderItem
├── BillingDocument / BillingDocumentItem
└── ProdOrderConfirmation

SQL (master orphaned as an island, lookup only):
└── Customer → name / country lookup via SQL CUSTOMER synonym

The W3C standards do not make this decision. The standard only says "create reference triples when FKs exist." "Should this entity live in the KG?" is a domain judgment.

3.1 Transactional data goes to SQL

The first decision is clear. "Data whose core is dates, amounts, and counts" does not belong in the KG.

Reasons not to load it into the KG:

  • Instance explosion: transactions are voluminous and change frequently. SalesOrder alone runs into tens or hundreds of thousands of rows. Graph nodes increase in proportion, weighing down both visualization and SPARQL query cost.
  • Numbers are natural in SQL: aggregations such as "revenue per customer" are much simpler and faster with SQL GROUP BY than with SPARQL.
  • Not a KG question: the KG exists to answer "what does this WorkCenter produce?" style structural traversals, not "what is the total?"

In our scenario, four DPs match these criteria:

DPWhy it is transactional
SalesOrder / SalesOrderItemSale date · amount · per-customer orders
PurchaseOrder / PurchaseOrderItemPurchase date · amount · per-supplier orders
BillingDocument / BillingDocumentItemBilling date · amount
ProductionOrderConfirmationProduction completion date · quantity

These four DPs move from master_data to transaction_data. The actual YAML update is handled together with the Section 3.2 island candidate check via a single Vibe Coding session at the end of Section 3.

📒 YAML changes from this step: the four DPs above move from master_data to transaction_data, and each DP's aggregation_columns is populated. Their entities are not persisted as KG nodes; they are used only as SQL aggregation targets. They remain in the YAML because the synonyms themselves are still needed for SQL queries (classification differs, but the synonyms persist).

3.2 Then master data that became an island also goes to SQL

After removing the transactional DPs in Section 3.1, looking again at the KG reveals an unexpected side effect. A piece of master data has become "an island."

In our scenario, Customer did. Customer is master data, and the demo data has roughly 215 instances. Initially, we naturally intended to place it in the KG. "Customers who buy Products" is a core relationship in the scenario.

But there was a problem. The bridge connecting Product and Customer is SalesOrder, and we removed SalesOrder from the KG in Section 3.1. The KG then looks like this:

With the bridge (SalesOrder) absent from the KG, the KG path from Product to Customer is severed. Even if Customer is persisted in the KG, it is an isolated island unconnected to other nodes. The Agent cannot navigate outward from a Customer instance via SPARQL, and other KG nodes cannot reach Customer. Roughly 200 instances would remain as an isolated collection of nodes.

So we decided to "handle the Product → Customer connection in SQL" (since SalesOrder is already in SQL, one SQL JOIN on SALES_ORDER_ITEM.Material / SoldToParty suffices), and moved Customer itself to a SQL lookup. Looking up name and country by ID is all that is required.

Even for master data, if it is not connected to other nodes within the KG, there is little reason to place it there. For simple lookup, SQL is simpler.

"Island candidate" signals

To make the same judgment in your own domain, the master data that will become "an island" becomes visible only after working through Section 3.1. Two signals:

  1. Is the bridge transactional, so it moved to SQL? If the only path connecting this master to other masters lies inside a transactional DP that moved to SQL in Section 3.1, placing it in the KG produces a disconnected node. (The DPs the LLM reports as "island candidates" at the end of the Section 3.1 Vibe Coding fall here.)
  2. Is lookup sufficient? If there is no "navigating outward" from this master to other entities via SPARQL (only looking up names and attributes by ID), a SQL lookup is simpler.

When both apply, moving to SQL is the natural choice.

⚠️ Limits of island detection: at design time, candidates only; final confirmation comes after running the Agent. The Section 3.1 Vibe Coding catches the structural signal "the bridge moved to SQL" automatically, but the usage signal "SPARQL never actually starts here" surfaces only after running the Agent. We too initially placed Customer in the KG, then after running the Z_ASM3 scenario and confirming that "it was never used as a starting point," moved it to SQL. The detailed case is covered in Part 5 Challenge.

📒 YAML changes from this step: masters confirmed as islands among the "island candidates" reported in Section 3.1 (in our case, Customer) drop out of the KG node set. They remain in the master_data category in the YAML, but they are not loaded as KG classes when authoring the Section 5 final ontology Turtle.

 

Vibe Coding: handling Section 3 in one shot (transaction classification + island candidate reporting)

The decisions of Section 3.1 and Section 3.2 are handled together in a single LLM prompt. Immediately after classifying transactional DPs, ask the LLM to check whether "any master data depended on a bridge that was just moved to transactional," and the Section 3.2 review goes much faster.

📥Input

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml (updated through Section 2 — master_data + structural_data)
  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md (per-DP amount and quantity columns documented)
  • CQ list

📤Output

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml updated: transactional DPs moved to transaction_data + each DP's aggregation_columns populated
  • Island candidate report: the list of master DPs (if any) disconnected from the KG as a result of the transactional moves
Examine ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml, docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md, and the CQ
list, and pick the "DPs that should be classified as transactional," moving
them into the transaction_data category.

Transaction criteria:
- DPs whose core is dates, amounts, and counts (consult the amount/quantity
  columns in CSN_SUMMARY)
- High volume, frequently changing (SalesOrder, PurchaseOrder, BillingDocument
  and similar)
- Appear in the CQs in "total / average / aggregation" form (numeric
  aggregation rather than structural traversal)

When moving:
- For each DP, populate aggregation_columns with the amount/quantity columns
  from CSN_SUMMARY
- Do NOT touch the master_data / structural_data classification (decided in
  earlier steps)

⚠️ Important: after moving transactional DPs, examine master_data once more.
If a master DP's only path to other master nodes ran through a transactional
DP that was just moved, that master will become an "island" in the KG.
If such a candidate exists, report it separately as a "KG island candidate"
(leave the YAML category as master_data for now — I will move it after
confirmation).

Finally:
- Which DPs moved to transaction_data + what aggregation_columns were populated
- If there are island candidates, which DP is suspected and why (which
  transactional DP was the bridge)
Summarize briefly and show me.

⚠️ After modifying the YAML, always confirm yaml.safe_load() parses
successfully, and if it breaks, briefly explain what went wrong, fix it,
and verify again.

A person reviews the LLM's "island candidates" and confirms which ones to remove from the KG node set (the YAML category stays as master_data; the entity simply is not persisted as an ontology class in Section 5).

Section 3 deliverables:

StepDeliverableRole
Transactional → SQL (Section 3.1)dp_mapping.yaml update (4 DPs to transaction_data)SQL aggregation targets confirmed
Islands → SQL (Section 3.2)dp_mapping.yaml update (island candidate entities excluded from KG nodes)Entities for the KG and entities for SQL-only confirmed

4. Splitting One Data Product into Multiple Classes (Class Partition)

After the KG vs SQL classification, the next frequently encountered domain decision is Class Partition.

SAP BDC has a single Product Data Product. HANA Cloud has a single corresponding Virtual Table (BDC_DP.PRODUCT). Inside that table are finished goods, semi-finished goods, and raw materials. The distinguishing field is MaterialType.

SELECT "MaterialType", COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM BDC_DP.PRODUCT
GROUP BY "MaterialType"
ORDER BY cnt DESC;

 

MaterialTypeSAP meaning
HALBSemi-finished (Halbfabrikat)
FERTFinished good (Fertigprodukt)
ROHRaw material (Rohmaterial)

Applied as-is, W3C Direct Mapping would map all three to a single bdc:Product. The data is stored. But we split it into three classes for two reasons.

Reason 1: the directionality of relationships differs

In a supply chain BOM, the flow is always one-directional.

WorkCenter ──producesProduct──▶ FinishedGood
FinishedGood ──hasComponent──▶ SemiFinished
SemiFinished ──hasComponent──▶ RawMaterial

If all three are bdc:Product, a SPARQL query for "what does Z_ASM3 produce?" may return a mix of raw materials and semi-finished goods. Type constraints enforce the correct answer.

Reason 2: an LLM reasons from class names

When the Agent sees ?p a bdc:FinishedGood in a SPARQL result, it knows immediately: this is a sellable end product, so look at sales orders and customers. For bdc:RawMaterial, look at procurement, suppliers, and purchase orders. The class name conveys business meaning, and the LLM uses that meaning to decide its next action.

The Class Partition pattern

In ontology design, this situation is called Class Partition or Discriminator-based Class Assignment. The pattern assigns different KG classes to rows of a single source (table) based on the value of a specific column (discriminator).

In OWL, this disjointness between classes can be declared explicitly.

# The three classes are mutually disjoint (a finished good cannot also be a raw material)
bdc:FinishedGood  owl:disjointWith bdc:SemiFinished .
bdc:FinishedGood  owl:disjointWith bdc:RawMaterial .
bdc:SemiFinished  owl:disjointWith bdc:RawMaterial .

# Or, with owl:disjointUnion (the three classes form a complete partition of Product)
bdc:Product owl:disjointUnion (bdc:FinishedGood bdc:SemiFinished bdc:RawMaterial) .

Signals that Class Partition should be considered

While exploring a Virtual Table, if any of these patterns appears, consider partitioning:

  • A type / category column exists: MaterialType, DocumentType, OrderType, and so on
  • Different row sets connect to different entities: some rows link to Supplier, others to Customer
  • Business users say "those are different things": "finished goods and raw materials are different."

This pattern appears often in BDC Data Products because S/4HANA table design relies on type columns for flexibility, storing multiple concepts in a single table. ODM surfaces these as meaningful names, but whether to split them into KG classes is the designer's decision.

Design rule: if different row sets participate in different relationships, split a single source table into multiple KG classes.

The Class Partition decision is made by a person

Do not delegate "split this table into classes" to the LLM wholesale. The LLM does not know the business meaning and will partition the wrong place, or merge a place that should be partitioned. Which of the three signals above (type column · different relationships per row set · business users distinguishing them) applies to your domain is something only a person knows.

Vibe Coding: Class Partition (two steps)

📥Input

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml: DP list under review + synonym (BDC_DP.<NAME>) and virtual_table_schema / name mapping
  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md: per-DP keys / relationship links / amount · quantity columns (supplementary)
  • HANA Cloud's PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS (column metadata) + BDC_DP.<SHORT_NAME> (row queries)

📤Output

  • Step 1: partition candidate table (DP · column · distinct values · row counts · expected connections) + first-pass recommendation
  • Step 2: partition block added to the relevant DP in dp_mapping.yaml (discriminator + subclass mapping)
  • In the next step (Section 6), when authoring ontology.ttl, this partition information is converted into Turtle class definitions
Good prompt / Step 1: find partition candidates

"For the DPs in the master_data and structural_data categories of
dp_mapping.yaml, organize type/category partition candidates.

[Input materials]
- ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml : DP list under review + synonym / VT name mapping
- docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md : supplementary — per-DP keys / relationship
  links / amount · quantity columns

Each DP entity has two names in the YAML, each with a different purpose:
- synonym (for example, BDC_DP.WORK_CENTER) : short name for SQL row/value queries
- virtual_table_schema + virtual_table_name : original names for catalog queries
  ⚠️ HANA Cloud has no DESCRIBE. Column information must be queried from
  PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS using virtual_table_schema/virtual_table_name (not
  the synonym).

What to organize (automatable):
1. The column list for each entity (query PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS)
2. Candidate columns that look like a type/category (MaterialType, DocumentType,
   OrderType, and so on)
3. For each candidate column, the distinct values + per-value row counts
   (SELECT <candidate>, COUNT(*) FROM BDC_DP.<SHORT_NAME> GROUP BY <candidate>
    — using the synonym)
4. For each distinct value, guess which other DP/column it likely connects to,
   and run a light feasibility check
   - The guess is based on column names and domain common sense (for example,
     MaterialType='FERT' → SalesOrderItem.Material likely)
   - Confirm only by comparing distinct counts on both columns. Do not run JOINs.
     If the distinct values on both columns overlap, report "likely connection."

Show the result as a table:
DP | candidate column | distinct values | per-value row count | expected
   connected DP·column | expected column's distinct count (+overlap signal).

After the table, add a single paragraph with a first-pass recommendation:
"Partitioning looks worthwhile for candidates X and Y. Reasons:
- X: all N distinct values look meaningful, per-value row counts are balanced,
  and the connected DPs differ by value (overlap pattern varies by value)
- Y: ...
Conversely, A and B look weak (for example, a single value covers 99%+, or
distinct count is too high suggesting an ID rather than a type column, or
every value has only 1–2 rows so domain meaning is weak)."

⚠️ SQL usage limit: stop at distinct + COUNT. Do not run JOINs.
- JOINs are not needed to find partition candidates (distinct comparison on
  both sides is enough)
- BDC Virtual Tables are external system calls, so heavy JOINs are costly."

→ Once the LLM produces the table and first-pass recommendation, the next stage is manual review by a person.

Manual review: do the type values play different business roles?

The LLM output above (column · distinct values · row counts · expected connections) shows only "the shape of a partition candidate." Judging the real partition value requires one more step.

The core question: does each type value play "a different business role"? There are two ways to look at this.

  1. Cross-reference distinct + COUNT results (light, default): examine the "expected connected DP · column" and "expected column's distinct count (+overlap signal)" in the LLM table, and check whether the connections differ per type value. Since this is based only on distinct and COUNT, it is light, and the BDC Virtual Table cost is small.
  2. Precise check with JOINs (only when needed): only when distinct · COUNT comparison cannot reveal the difference. For example, "both columns are 4-character codes with similar distinct counts; I want the actual matching row count" or "one side has trailing spaces, so distinct counts look identical but the JOIN match rate may be low." In these cases, use a verification script such as 02_verify_joins.py to confirm match rates and NULL ratios directly.

Example: examining the MaterialType candidate of the Product DP

 

MaterialTypeHow it is referenced in other DPsBusiness role
FERT (finished good)Large distinct overlap with SalesOrderItem.MaterialSold
ROH (raw material)Large distinct overlap with PurchaseOrderItem.Material / PurchasingSourceList.MaterialPurchased
HALB (semi-finished)Appears in Product.hasComponent (internal BOM)Internal assembly

If the three values show different connection patterns → different business roles → partition is worthwhile. If the three values show the same connection pattern → effectively the same role → partition value is weak, a single class suffices.

This judgment cannot be made by the LLM. With the LLM result table in hand, a person decides "are these type values really different roles?" Our scenario's Product followed exactly this analysis and led to the decision "three roles are clearly different → split into three classes."

Good prompt / Step 2: persist the decision as a YAML partition

"Take the Step 1 results and the partition decision I confirmed, and add a
partition block to the relevant DP in dp_mapping.yaml.

Confirmed partition (example):
- DP: BDC_DP.PRODUCT
- discriminator column: MaterialType
- value → class mapping:
    HALB → bdc:SemiFinished
    FERT → bdc:FinishedGood
    ROH  → bdc:RawMaterial

Deliverable: partition block added to dp_mapping.yaml
- A partition block at the same level as the relevant DP entity (per the
  template):
    - name: Product
      kg_class: bdc:Product
      entities:
        - entity: Product
          synonym_name: PRODUCT
          ...
      partition:
        discriminator: MaterialType
        parent_class: bdc:Product
        disjoint: true
        subclasses:
          - value: FERT
            kg_class: bdc:FinishedGood
            description: "Finished good"
          - value: HALB
            kg_class: bdc:SemiFinished
            description: "Semi-finished"
          - value: ROH
            kg_class: bdc:RawMaterial
            description: "Raw material"
- Output form: show the updated DP block as it should appear in the current YAML

⚠️ Do NOT create Turtle class definitions (rdfs:label, rdfs:comment,
rdfs:subClassOf, owl:disjointWith) in this step. The ontology.ttl authoring
step will take the YAML partition as input and generate them in one pass.

⚠️ In this step, persist only the partition block. Do not touch other regions
of master_data (entities, datatype_properties), structural_data,
transaction_data, or relationships. In particular, datatype_properties will
be organized separately in the next step.

[Memo for relationships narrowed by the partition (if any)]
If the partition result allows the range/domain of an existing relationship
candidate to be narrowed (for example, the range of producesProduct can be
refined from bdc:Product to the partition subclass bdc:FinishedGood), add
that information as a free-form description memo to the relationships
section of the YAML. Use the same tone (one-line description) as other
candidates already persisted there. Formal definitions will come in the
ontology design step.

Example:
relationships:
  - description: "WorkCenter → Product via routing chain (matched: 9,200 rows)"  # JOIN verification candidate
  - description: "[partition memo] The range of producesProduct from WorkCenter can be narrowed to bdc:FinishedGood (Product partition result)"  # partition memo

If no new relationship memos arise from the partition, skip this step.

⚠️ After modifying the YAML, always confirm yaml.safe_load() parses
successfully, and if it breaks, briefly explain what went wrong, fix it,
and verify again."
Bad prompt:

"Split the classes in this ontology appropriately."
→ "Appropriately" has no criterion. The LLM partitions by guesswork.
→ Information-carrying nodes such as BOMItem get oversimplified, losing
   information.

Roles:

  • Domain expert: decides whether to partition, which column acts as the discriminator, and which class names to use
  • LLM: identifying candidates from metadata (Step 1), persisting the decision as a YAML partition block (Step 2)

📒 YAML changes from this step: a partition block is added to the relevant DP entry. The existing kg_class (parent class, for example bdc:Product) stays, and the discriminator column, the subclass mapping, and the disjoint flag are added. The Turtle class definitions themselves are generated when authoring ontology.ttl in the next step, reading this YAML.

Section 4 deliverables:

 

StepDeliverableRole
Class Partition decisiondp_mapping.yaml update (discriminator partition rules + per-entity kg_class)Input for Section 6 ontology Turtle authoring

5. Completing the Ontology

The KG ontology structure that emerges after applying every decision in Sections 3 and 4 is below. This structure is consistent with the final classification in dp_mapping.yaml. master_data becomes the bdc:MasterData hierarchy, PSL (the structural item with its own attributes) sits under bdc:StructuralNode, and DPs that act only as intermediate bridges, such as ProductionRouting, have no KG nodes and serve only as KGR View inputs (Part 3). If the YAML was the working log, the ontology TTL is the result of translating that log into W3C standard syntax.

Instance counts below are SAP demo data examples.

rdfs:Class
├── bdc:MasterData
│   ├── bdc:FinishedGood     , finished good (225 examples)
│   ├── bdc:SemiFinished     , semi-finished (2,185 examples)
│   ├── bdc:RawMaterial      , raw material (183 examples)
│   ├── bdc:Supplier         , supplier (153 examples)
│   ├── bdc:Plant            , plant (12 examples)
│   └── bdc:WorkCenter       , work center (129 examples)
│
└── bdc:StructuralNode
    └── bdc:PurchasingSourceList , Supplier ↔ Product bridge (16 examples)

Relationships:

WorkCenter   ──bdc:locatedAt──▶         Plant
WorkCenter   ──bdc:producesProduct──▶   FinishedGood
Product      ──bdc:locatedAt──▶         Plant
Product      ──bdc:hasComponent──▶      Product
PSL          ──bdc:forProduct──▶        Product
PSL          ──bdc:fromSupplier──▶      Supplier

PurchasingSourceList (PSL) is intentionally classified as StructuralNode rather than MasterData. PSL is not a simple N:N connection; it carries information such as "at which plant, for which validity period, with which priority." The decision was to keep meaningful junctions as graph nodes rather than flattening them.

📌 These relationships are realized as KGR Views in Part 3. bdc:producesProduct, which requires compressing a multi-hop chain; bdc:hasComponent, which traverses a BOM; bdc:locatedAt, a simple direct FK. Each is precomputed as a SQL View (KGR_*) that becomes input to instance TTL generation. The View patterns are covered in detail in Part 3 Section 3.

Vibe Coding: organizing relationships in the YAML

After Section 2 JOIN verification and Section 4 Class Partition, free-form description memos for candidates have accumulated in the relationships section of the YAML. In this step, those are organized in one pass into the formal ObjectProperty form (name / domain / range / source).

📥Input

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml: the relationships section with Section 2 JOIN verification candidates + Section 4 partition memos accumulated as free-form descriptions, plus the master_data / structural_data / partition regions
  • The Section 5 ontology diagram above (the confirmed relationship list — which relationships to keep or drop, and what to call them)
  • The verification results from ingestion/02_verify_joins.py (per-candidate JOIN pattern and matching row count) — the basis for authoring SQL View bodies

📤Output

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml update: free-form items in relationships replaced with the formal form (name / domain / range / source)
  • ingestion/kgr_views/<view_name lowercase>.sql files — the View body for each relationship (only the SELECT portion of CREATE VIEW)
  • Change description (which free-form candidate became which formal relationship)
Organize the relationships section of ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml.

Free-form description candidates persisted in earlier steps (JOIN verification +
Class Partition) have accumulated there. In this step, replace them in one pass
with the formal ObjectProperty form.

The ontology diagram I confirmed (relationship list):
- WorkCenter → bdc:locatedAt → Plant
- WorkCenter → bdc:producesProduct → bdc:FinishedGood  (※ range narrowed by partition)
- Product → bdc:locatedAt → Plant
- Product → bdc:hasComponent → Product
- PurchasingSourceList → bdc:forProduct → Product
- PurchasingSourceList → bdc:fromSupplier → Supplier
... (adjust to your domain)

Workflow:
1. Read every free-form description candidate in yaml relationships and match
   each to the confirmed list above. (Which candidate maps to which relationship.)
2. Replace relationships in the confirmed list with the formal form:
   - name : relationship name (camelCase)
   - domain : subject class
   - range : object class (use the narrowed subclass if a partition memo exists)
   - description : one-line description
   - source.type : kgr_view
   - source.view_name : convention KGR_<SUBJ>_<REL>_<OBJ>
   - source.sql_file : convention ingestion/kgr_views/<view_name lowercase>.sql
3. Remove free-form candidates that are not in the confirmed list. (Verified
   but decided against persisting in the ontology.)
4. For each formal relationship, author the actual SQL View body at the
   source.sql_file path:
   - Use the JOIN pattern from 02_verify_joins.py verification results
   - The file contains only the SELECT portion of CREATE VIEW (the 03 script
     wraps the CREATE VIEW statement)
   - Output columns: (subject_iri, object_iri) only. For multi-hop, route
     through intermediate JOINs and SELECT only the two final columns. Apply
     DISTINCT.
   - subject_iri / object_iri are IRI strings of the form 'bdc:<Class>_' ||
     <key column> (for example, 'bdc:WorkCenter_' || wc.WorkCenterInternalID)

⚠️ Do NOT touch master_data / structural_data / transaction_data / partition /
   datatype_properties. They were decided in earlier steps or are scheduled
   for the next step. Organize the relationships section only.

⚠️ After modifying the YAML, always confirm yaml.safe_load() parses
   successfully, and if it breaks, briefly explain what went wrong, fix it,
   and verify again.

Finally, show the result in a table:
| free-form candidate (before) | formal relationship (after) | view_name | sql_file | notes |

The deliverables of this step come in pairs. In the YAML, relationships[*].source points to the view that realizes each relationship and to the .sql file that contains the view body. The actual SQL files are stored under ingestion/kgr_views/, one file per view. In the next post, Part 3, the KGR view creation script reads these YAML paths and loads each SQL body into HANA as a CREATE VIEW statement.

Vibe Coding: organizing datatype_properties in the YAML

With the classes ready, the next task is to persist which columns will become attributes (owl:DatatypeProperty) on each class. The "what to persist" decision goes into the YAML; the actual Turtle definitions are produced by the LLM in Section 6 from the YAML.

📥Input

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml: the six DPs in master_data (with relationships now populated)
  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md: column list + business meaning per DP
  • HANA PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS: DATA_TYPE_NAME per column (for XSD type mapping)

📤Output

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml update: master_data[*].datatype_properties populated for each DP (column / property / range)
  • Report of omitted / excluded columns (with reasons)
Populate datatype_properties for each DP under master_data in
ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml.

[Input materials]
- ingestion/dp_mapping.template.yaml : the datatype_properties form + XSD
  mapping rules are documented as comments. Follow that form exactly.
- ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml : kg_class and partition for each DP under
  master_data are now decided
- docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md : column list + business meaning per DP
- Direct query of HANA PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS : XSD type mapping based on
  DATA_TYPE_NAME of rows whose SCHEMA_NAME is each DP's virtual_table_schema

[XSD type mapping rules]
- NVARCHAR / VARCHAR / STRING            → xsd:string
- INTEGER / BIGINT / SMALLINT / TINYINT  → xsd:integer
- DECIMAL / DOUBLE / REAL / FLOAT        → xsd:decimal
- DATE                                    → xsd:date
- TIMESTAMP / SECONDDATE                  → xsd:dateTime
- BOOLEAN                                 → xsd:boolean

[Column selection rules]
Include:
- Name / Description (label · comment candidates)
- business codes (ProductType, MaterialGroup, and so on)
- amount / quantity / count
- business dates (ValidFrom / ValidTo / DocumentDate, and so on)

Exclude:
- GUID / UUID (for example, ProductUUID, *_GUID)
- audit metadata (CreatedAt / CreatedBy / LastChangedAt / LastChangedBy)
- ETag / Version (for example, SAP_EntityChangeDate, SAP_ETag)
- soft-delete flags (IsDeleted / IsArchived / DeletionIndicator)
- FK columns (already persisted as ObjectProperties in relationships)

[Form]
Add a datatype_properties list to each DP under master_data:
  datatype_properties:
    - column: "<source column name, e.g., WorkCenterName>"
      property: "<bdc:camelCase, e.g., bdc:workCenterName>"
      range: "<XSD type, e.g., xsd:string>"

⚠️ Do NOT touch master_data / structural_data / transaction_data / partition /
   relationships. Add only master_data[*].datatype_properties.

⚠️ For DPs with a partition (for example, Product), persist
   datatype_properties at the parent class level (bdc:Product). Do not
   persist them separately per subclass (FinishedGood / SemiFinished /
   RawMaterial).

[Self-verification]
After modifying the YAML, confirm yaml.safe_load() parses. If it breaks,
fix it and verify again.

Finally, report:
- How many columns were added per DP
- The list of excluded columns + reason (GUID / audit / etc.)

A person scans the reported exclusion list and confirms only whether "this column is actually domain-important and was dropped" by mistake. In most cases, the rules apply cleanly.

The next Section 6 covers how this ontology structure is delegated to the LLM to produce the draft Turtle, and how labels and comments are written.

Section 5 deliverables:

StepDeliverableRole
Final ontology structure confirmedOntology diagram (6 MasterData + 1 StructuralNode + 6 relationships)Blueprint for Section 6 Turtle authoring
relationships organizeddp_mapping.yaml's relationships section replaced from Section 2 / Section 4 free-form candidates with the formal form (name / domain / range / source)Direct input for Section 6 Turtle authoring
KGR view SQL bodies authoredingestion/kgr_views/<view_name>.sql files (one file per view, the SELECT body)The Part 3 KGR view creation script reads them and loads them into HANA as CREATE VIEW
datatype_properties organizeddp_mapping.yaml's master_data[*].datatype_properties populated (column / property / range)Direct input for Section 6 Turtle authoring

6. Drafting the Ontology Turtle with AI, and Writing Labels

Working through Sections 2–5 accumulated every ontology decision in dp_mapping.yaml: category classification, VT / synonym mapping, kg_class, partition, and relationships. In this step, that YAML is converted to W3C standard Turtle format, while semantic vocabulary such as rdfs:label, rdfs:comment, and skos:altLabel is added in parallel.

The key point is that a person does not maintain the entity list in their head. The YAML is the SSoT of decisions, so a single LLM prompt, "convert this YAML to Turtle", produces the draft.

📥Input (entire Section 6)

  • ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml: the SSoT accumulated across Sections 2–5 (category, kg_class, synonym, partition, relationships)
  • docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md: columns, amounts, and quantities per DP — reference for the domain vocabulary in DatatypeProperty and label / comment
  • (Optional) 02_verify_joins.py verification report: evidence that relationships are alive. Reference for memos such as row counts in comments

📤Output (entire Section 6)

  • ingestion/04_generate_ontology.py: a Python script that persists the TTL text as a multi-line string and writes it to ingestion/ontology/bdc_dp_ontology.ttl (using only pathlib, no external libraries such as rdflib)
  • ingestion/ontology/bdc_dp_ontology.ttl: the script output — classes + ObjectProperty + DatatypeProperty + multilingual labels and comments + skos:altLabel synonyms
  • Used in: Part 3 Section 5 TTL generation (loaded into HANA KGE)

Vibe Coding: YAML → ontology.ttl generation

Write a Python script ingestion/04_generate_ontology.py that takes
ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml as input and generates a W3C Turtle ontology.

[Output form]
- Persist the entire TTL inside the Python script as a multi-line string
  (ONTOLOGY = \"\"\"...\"\"\") and write it to
  ingestion/ontology/bdc_dp_ontology.ttl.
- Use only pathlib, no external libraries. Do NOT use rdflib for builder
  code. Author the TTL text yourself and persist it as a string.

[Input materials]
- ingestion/dp_mapping.yaml : the SSoT of every ontology decision
  - master_data[*].kg_class : class name
  - master_data[*].partition : Class Partition (generate subclasses if present)
  - master_data[*].datatype_properties : DatatypeProperty definitions (column,
    property, range)
  - structural_data[*].kg_class : StructuralNode hierarchy
  - relationships[*] : ObjectProperty definitions (name, domain, range,
    description)
- docs/dp_specs/CSN_SUMMARY.md : reference for label / comment domain
  vocabulary
  (the names and types of datatype_properties items in the YAML are already
   decided; only the label / comment phrasing in business vocabulary comes
   from here)

Authoring rules:
1. Prefix declarations (bdc:, rdfs:, rdf:, owl:, skos:, xsd:)
2. Class hierarchy:
   - kg_class values in master_data are rdfs:subClassOf bdc:MasterData
   - kg_class values in structural_data are subclasses of bdc:StructuralNode
   - DPs with a partition have the subclasses under partition.parent_class as
     rdfs:subClassOf; if partition.disjoint=true, also owl:disjointWith
3. Add rdfs:label and rdfs:comment to every class (multilingual: @en, @ko)
4. Define each item in relationships as owl:ObjectProperty
   (domain / range / label / comment)
5. Define each item in master_data[*].datatype_properties as
   owl:DatatypeProperty:
   - bdc:<property> a owl:DatatypeProperty ;
       rdfs:domain bdc:<kg_class> ; rdfs:range <range> ;
       rdfs:label ... ; rdfs:comment ... .
   - Take label / comment from the column descriptions in CSN_SUMMARY
     (multilingual @en/@ko).

⚠️ Do NOT add skos:altLabel (synonyms) in this step. Handled separately.
⚠️ Follow the YAML exactly. Do not add relationships or classes freely.
⚠️ Do NOT persist implementation metadata (view_name / sql_file) from
   relationships[*].source into the Turtle. The ontology contains semantics
   only (domain / range / label / comment). Implementation assets (views,
   SQL) are managed in separate .sql files.

[Self-verification — not inside the script, but as your own self-check]
Run the script once and parse the generated TTL with rdflib.Graph().parse()
to confirm it parses. If it breaks, briefly explain what went wrong, fix it,
and verify again. Hand over the final version in a parsing-passed state.

Roles:

  • LLM: authoring Turtle from the YAML, maintaining label · comment · domain · range consistency, multilingual expression
  • Domain expert: skims the class hierarchy and relationship direction in a visualization tool to confirm they match the YAML decisions, and reviews the business tone and multilingual phrasing of labels and comments

Reviewing the ontology with visualization / modeling tools

A person reviews the tone and multilingual phrasing of labels and comments. The class hierarchy, ObjectProperty definitions, and domain / range constraints were decided in the YAML, so the LLM's output can be trusted as-is.

Before loading the draft from the LLM, a visual review is a safety net. Confirm graphically that the class hierarchy, ObjectProperty direction, and domain / range constraints came out as intended. A few options:

  • Protégé: open-source ontology editor developed at Stanford. Opens a TTL file and shows the class hierarchy and ObjectProperty as a tree. Useful for a light review.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine: query the loaded KG directly with SPARQL for verification. Because ontology and instances live on a single platform, post-load verification is fast.
  • Metaphactory: an Enterprise KG platform officially integrated with SAP HANA Cloud KGE. Supports ontology editing, visual modeling, data entry, and SPARQL queries in a single interface. Suited for operating an ontology in a collaborative environment.

In a production project, ontology design and long-term maintenance should be done with modeling and governance tools such as Protégé or Metaphactory. The AI-based workflow in this post is not intended to replace those tools. Its value is that it creates a strong first draft quickly, especially when you are exploring a new domain or learning how SAP BDC metadata maps into KG classes and relationships. For scenario-based projects, this first draft is often sufficient to validate the core classes, relationships, and Competency Questions before formalizing the ontology in dedicated modeling tools. Once you gain experience with this workflow, you will be better prepared to review, refine, and operate the ontology in those tools. This is also aligned with where the tooling market is moving: modeling environments increasingly use AI to assist semantic modeling, vocabulary creation, and relationship discovery.

In our project, we reviewed with Protégé, then generated the final TTL with 04_generate_ontology.py (covered in detail in the next post, Part 3).

Synonyms: applying skos:altLabel in practice

Part 2-1 Section 1 covered the standards basis of the skos:altLabel pattern. Here we examine how it is applied in practice.

Situations where synonyms are needed:

  • When the Agent must link diverse expressions in user questions — "공급사," "벤더," "Vendor," "Supplier" — to the same class
  • When context_loader.py injects ontology metadata into the LLM, so that the LLM generates the correct SPARQL regardless of the user's vocabulary
@prefix skos: <http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#> .

bdc:Supplier a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label    "Supplier"@en ,
                  "공급사"@ko ;
    skos:altLabel "공급업체"@ko ,
                  "벤더"@ko ,
                  "Vendor"@en ;
    rdfs:comment  "A counterparty that supplies materials or services."@en .

This is the step deliberately omitted from the first Vibe Coding. Synonyms depend on industry, domain, and in-house vocabulary, so handing them off in a separate pass after the ontology draft is in place produces better quality.

🎯 Vibe Coding: bulk addition of skos:altLabel synonyms

Purpose: add user vocabulary variants (synonyms) to the ontology draft from the first Vibe Coding as skos:altLabel

Input:

  • ingestion/04_generate_ontology.py (the first Vibe Coding deliverable — the TTL string is persisted as a multi-line string)
  • Domain context (SAP industry vocabulary / in-house jargon / English vs Korean phrasing differences)

Deliverable: updated 04_generate_ontology.py — skos:altLabel triples added to the TTL string and @prefix skos: added to the top prefix block. Re-running updates bdc_dp_ontology.ttl.

Edit the TTL string (ONTOLOGY = \"\"\"...\"\"\") inside
ingestion/04_generate_ontology.py directly to add skos:altLabel synonyms to
each class. The targets are every class in master_data and structural_data
plus the subclasses of every Class Partition.

Rules:
1. Add @prefix skos: <http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#> . to the top prefix
   block
2. Leave rdfs:label as the "official name"; add only the other expressions
   users actually use as skos:altLabel
3. If both Korean and English variants are possible, add both (keep @ko / @en
   language tags)
4. SAP industry vocabulary (for example, Supplier → Vendor/벤더/거래처, Plant →
   공장/사업장, BillOfMaterial → BOM/명세서)
5. If you are uncertain for a given class, skip it and report which classes
   were skipped at the end
6. Do NOT introduce external libraries such as rdflib. Edit the existing
   multi-line string directly.

[Self-verification]
After re-running the script, parse the generated TTL once with
rdflib.Graph().parse() to confirm. If it breaks, fix and verify again. Hand
over the final version.

A person reviews only whether industry-specific terms or in-house jargon were omitted. If the LLM reports any "skipped classes," fill in the in-house vocabulary directly and run one more pass.

Synonyms are possible without SKOS. A custom property, or packing every variant into rdfs:label, also makes search work. The reason to use skos:altLabel is not to enable search; it is two-fold: the preferred / alternative role distinction is standardized and built in, and when later extending into cross-system mapping (skos:exactMatch and similar), the same vocabulary family is reused. For the detailed trade-off, consult Part 2-1 Section 1, "Can synonyms work without SKOS?"

Why this work is done at the ontology stage: it is reused in the Agent

Labels and comments are not just readability aids. They are the input that is injected as domain vocabulary into the LLM in the Part 4 Agent:

  • Ontology metadata loaded at Agent startup: class labels and comments are persisted in the system prompt so the LLM learns "bdc:WorkCenter is a work center located in a Plant, producing Finished Goods" → smarter SPARQL authoring
  • SPARQL result interpretation: with only URIs (bdc:WorkCenter_Z_ASM3), the LLM cannot read; with a label "Z_ASM3", it generates an answer using the business name directly
  • Multilingual answers: a Korean question prompts the Agent to retrieve the @KO label / comment first and answer in Korean, and English for English speakers. Multilingual support is delivered through the ontology alone, without backend code changes.

→ The quality of labels and comments at this stage is the quality of the Agent's answers. "Polish them later" is not the right approach; persist them alongside the ontology load. That is the standard.

Section 6 deliverables:

StepDeliverableRole
Ontology draftingestion/ontology/bdc_dp_ontology.ttl (draft)Classes + ObjectProperty + DatatypeProperty definitions
Multilingual label · comment authoringbdc_dp_ontology.ttl (final)@en / @ko multilingual labels and comments + skos:altLabel synonyms

→ In the next post (Part 3), this bdc_dp_ontology.ttl is loaded into SAP HANA Cloud KGE.

Summary

This post covered:

  • KG vs SQL classification: structural relationship traversal → KG, numeric aggregation → SQL. Entities with no outgoing relationships (Customer) become SQL lookups
  • Class Partition: separating different kinds of entities within a single table (Product by MaterialType) into distinct classes. Relationship directionality + LLM reasoning
  • Final ontology: two branches (MasterData / StructuralNode), six master classes + one bridge (PSL)
  • Drafting with AI: SAP ODM vocabulary lets the LLM produce a strong draft
  • rdfs:label authoring rules: applied to every instance, class, and property. The minimum requirement that makes the KG readable by the LLM

The deliverable of this post is a single file, bdc_dp_ontology.ttl , the blueprint of the KG. The next post covers the pipeline that fills that blueprint with actual data.

What's Next

The blueprint is complete. What remains is reading actual data from SAP BDC Data Products, transforming it into the KG, and loading it into SAP HANA Cloud KGE.

Part 3 – Data Pipeline from BDC to Triple Store starts with metadata exploration using PUBLIC.VIRTUAL_COLUMNS, covers the relationship SQL View pattern that compresses multi-hop relationships into 1-hop, the TTL generation pipeline, and the Triple Store inside SAP HANA Cloud KGE.

Stack: SAP BDC Data Products · HANA Cloud KGE · FastAPI · React · Claude (Anthropic / AI Core) Code: github.com/claudiopark86/sap-bdc-kge-agent-workshop

References

  • Ontology Development 101: A Guide to Creating Your First Ontology — Noy & McGuinness, Stanford 2001. https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf
  • A Direct Mapping of Relational Data to RDF — W3C Recommendation, 2012-09-27. https://www.w3.org/TR/rdb-direct-mapping/
  • R2RML: RDB to RDF Mapping Language — W3C Recommendation, 2012-09-27. https://www.w3.org/TR/r2rml/
  • OWL 2 Quick Reference Guide — W3C Recommendation, 2012-12-11. (§2.7 Annotations lists rdfs:label and rdfs:comment as built-in annotation properties.) https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-quick-reference/
  • SAP HANA Cloud Integration with Metaphactory for Knowledge Graph Modelling — SAP Community Blog. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-hana-cloud-integration-with-metaphactory-for-knowledge-graph-modelling/ba-p/14390776
  • HANA Cloud Data Products Consumption — SAP Tutorial. https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/hana-cloud-data-products-consumption.html
  • Data Product Support in SAP HANA Cloud — SAP Help Portal. https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-administration-guide/data-product-support-in-sap-hana-cloud-internal?locale=en-US

entry.title: Code Connect 2026: Final Info Before We Kick Off!
entry.content:
Code Connect 2026 is just around the corner! From July 13–16, 2026, the SAP developer community will gather in St. Leon-Rot, Germany – onsite and online.

Here’s everything you need to know before we get started.

 

 

We Are Fully Booked!

All onsite tickets are fully booked - thank you for the amazing interest!

If you couldn’t secure a ticket:

  • Join the waiting lists on Code Connect 2026 in case seats become available.
  • Follow selected sessions via live stream or Microsoft Teams link.

Even if you're not onsite, you can still be part of Code Connect. Selected sessions will be streamed live, and recordings of some sessions will be available afterwards. Check back soon for details.

 

 

 

UI5con (July 14)

  • Main stage sessions will be live streamed on YouTube.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are streamed.

 

 

re>≡CAP (July 15)

  • Main stage sessions will be broadcasted.
  • Some sidetracks (W1/W2) can be attended via Microsoft Teams link.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are broadcasted or available via Microsoft Teams link.

 

 

HANA Tech Con (July 16)

  • The links will be announced on the event day.

 

The Agendas Are Live

The full program is already available - time to plan your schedule!

Check-In opens at 8.00 AM every day. Please bring the QR code with you that you can find in your ticket.

July 13 – Code Jams & Community Meetup

  • Hands-on Code Jams:
    • OpenUI5
    • CAP
    • AI Agents
  • Informal pre-event meetup (no registration needed):
    Ihle Besen, Höfe am Sträßel 3, 69231 Rauenberg
    5:00 PM CEST

July 14–16 – Main Conference Days

Explore all agendas:

  • July 14: UI5con
  • July 15: re>≡CAP
  • July 16: HANA Tech Con

On July 14 also the HANA AI CodeJam takes place.

 

Location

Code Connect takes place at:

SAP-Allee 27
68789 St. Leon-Rot, Germany

Find detailed information on directions, parking, and public transport here.

 

Final Tips

  • Bookmark your favorite sessions in advance
  • Arrive early for popular sessions (rooms fill quickly!)
  • Bring your laptop for Code Jams and hands-on sessions/ workshops
  • Don’t miss the networking moments - some of the best conversations happen in between sessions
  • Follow the agendas closely for last-minute updates

 

See You Soon!

We’re looking forward to an inspiring week of learning, sharing, and connecting.

See you at Code Connect 2026!

entry.title: Building Vector Search Applications with SAP HANA Cloud and LangChain.js
entry.content:
Imagine your customer types “something comfortable for working from home” into your shop’s search bar. There’s no “comfortable” column in your product table. No “work from home” tag either. And yet your store somehow needs to surface the ergonomic chair, the noise-cancelling headphones, and the standing desk.

That gap between what people say and what your database stores is exactly where vector search lives. With @SAP/hana-langchain, your SAP HANA Cloud database doesn’t just store your data. It understands it.

The full walkthrough, with every line of code, every output, and every config option, lives on GitHub: blogs/blog-post.md. The sections below focus on the ideas worth remembering, paired with the bits of code and output that bring them to life.

What it is

@SAP/hana-langchain is the official open-source bridge between SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine and LangChain.js. It turns your enterprise database into a first-class AI retrieval layer for RAG, semantic search, recommendations, and intelligent Q&A, all running where your data already lives.

Two lines and you have a vector store:

npm install @langchain/core @langchain/classic langchain
npm install @sap/hana-langchain

Searching by intent, not by keywords

Let’s walk through the real story. A customer is setting up their home office. They open your store and search:

“comfortable work from home setup”

Your catalogue has hundreds of products. Keyword search? Zero relevant hits. With HanaDB? Watch what happens.

import { HanaDB, HanaInternalEmbeddings } from "@sap/hana-langchain";

// Embeddings happen INSIDE HANA. No external API. No data leaving your DB.
const embeddings = new HanaInternalEmbeddings({
  internalEmbeddingModelId: "SAP_NEB.20240715",
});

const store = new HanaDB(embeddings, {
  connection: client,
  tableName: "PRODUCT_CATALOG",
});
await store.initialize();

const results = await store.similaritySearch(
  "comfortable work from home setup",
  2,
);

The output:

[
  Document {
    pageContent: 'Ergonomic office chair with lumbar support and adjustable armrests',
    metadata: { category: 'furniture', price: 449, in_stock: true }
  },
  Document {
    pageContent: 'Professional espresso machine with built-in grinder',
    metadata: { category: 'appliances', price: 899.99, in_stock: false }
  }
]

The chair makes obvious sense. The espresso machine? Also surprisingly on-brand for “working from home.” The model picked up on intent that no SQL LIKE ever could.

Cosine vs. Euclidean: pick your distance

So how does similaritySearch actually decide which products are “closest” to the query? Each document and the query are turned into vectors, and then HanaDB applies a distance metric to measure how far apart they are. The library supports two: cosine (the default) and Euclidean. You pick which one you want with a single config option when you create the store.

The choice matters because the two metrics score the same set of candidates differently. Take the query “high quality audio headphones” and run it under both strategies. Same data, same embedding model, different scoring philosophy:

COSINE Similarity Results (higher = more similar)
[0.7219] Wireless noise-canceling headphones with premium sound quality
[0.5506] Professional studio microphone for podcasting and streaming
[0.5206] Bluetooth speaker with deep bass and 20-hour battery

EUCLIDEAN Distance Results (lower = more similar)
[0.7086] Wireless noise-canceling headphones with premium sound quality
[0.9048] Professional studio microphone for podcasting and streaming
[0.9251] Bluetooth speaker with deep bass and 20-hour battery

Both metrics pick the same winner here, but notice the gap between first and second place: cosine spreads the candidates by relative similarity, Euclidean by raw distance in vector space. As a rule of thumb, cosine is the right default for text, because it only cares about the direction the vectors point in, not how long they are. Euclidean is a better fit when the actual position of the vectors carries meaning, for example when your embeddings come from numerical features rather than text. If you’re not sure which to pick, leave it on cosine.

Adding diversity when you need it: MMR

Sometimes “most similar” is exactly what the user wants. A shopper who searches for “wireless headphones” expects a list of wireless headphones, and similarity search delivers that perfectly well.

But there are spots in the same product journey where pure similarity hurts the experience. The classic case is the “customers also viewed” strip on a product detail page. A shopper looking at a premium open-back studio headphone doesn’t need to see four more premium over-ear headphones below it. They’d be much better served by a strip that also shows them a different form factor, a different price tier, or a noise-cancelling alternative.

That’s the job of Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR). Instead of returning the top-k most similar documents, MMR picks results that are both relevant and different from each other. You retrieve a larger candidate pool; MMR walks it, picking the next item that maximises relevance while penalising redundancy with what’s already been chosen. One lambda knob tunes the balance between relevance and diversity.

Same vector store, same query, two different methods. First, plain similarity search:

// Plain similarity search: top-4 candidates ordered by closeness alone
const similar = await vectorStore.similaritySearch(
  "premium wireless over-ear headphones",
  4,
);
similaritySearch top 4:
  1. AudioMax Pro 5     (over-ear-wireless, $349.99)
  2. AuroraSound Pro    (over-ear-wireless, $379.99)
  3. AudioMax Buds 5    (true-wireless,     $299.99)
  4. QuietShield Ultra  (over-ear-wireless, $429.99)

Three of the four results are premium over-ear wireless headphones, almost interchangeable for the shopper. Not a useful “customers also viewed” strip.

Now the same query, through MMR:

// MMR: pick 4 from a wider candidate pool while penalising redundancy.
// lambda = 0.5 balances relevance and diversity evenly.
const recommendations = await vectorStore.maxMarginalRelevanceSearch(
  "premium wireless over-ear headphones",
  { k: 4, fetchK: 12, lambda: 0.5 },
);
maxMarginalRelevanceSearch top 4:
  1. AudioMax Pro 5     (over-ear-wireless, $349.99)
  2. FlexFit Sport      (neckband,           $89.99)
  3. AuroraSound Pro    (over-ear-wireless, $379.99)
  4. TuneCore 510       (on-ear-wireless,    $49.99)

The top match is still the most relevant premium over-ear pair, but the next three deliberately span different form factors and price tiers. The shopper now sees an actual mix to compare, all from one method swap on the same vector store and the same product catalogue.

Use similaritySearch when the goal is the closest match. Reach for maxMarginalRelevanceSearch when the goal is coverage: recommendation strips, RAG context that spans multiple sub-topics, related-articles modules, or anywhere a single dominant theme would crowd out everything else.

Filtering that speaks JavaScript

Semantic search is great. Semantic search plus structured filters is unbeatable. HanaDB’s filter syntax will feel instantly familiar to anyone who’s ever written a MongoDB query:

// In-stock electronics under $500
const filter = {
  $and: [
    { in_stock: true },
    { category: "electronics" },
    { price: { $lt: 500 } },
  ],
};

const results = await store.similaritySearch("best audio quality", 10, filter);

And the result is exactly what the filter promised: only in-stock electronics, all under $500, ranked by audio quality:

[
  { pageContent: 'Premium wireless headphones with spatial audio...',  price: 349.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Budget wireless earbuds with decent sound...',       price:  49.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Bluetooth over-ear headphones with active noise...', price: 249.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Wireless noise-canceling headphones with 30-hour...',price: 299.99, in_stock: true }
]

You get the full operator suite ($lt, $lte, $gte, $between, $in, $nin, $like, $contains, $and, $or) all compiled down to native HANA SQL. No JavaScript-side filtering. No surprises at scale.

Letting the LLM write its own filters

Here’s where it gets fun. What if a user types “Show me AudioMax electronics under $400 with good ratings”? You could build a custom intent parser. Or you could let the LLM do it for free, with the HanaTranslator:

const retriever = SelfQueryRetriever.fromLLM({
  llm,
  vectorStore: store,
  documentContents: "Product description for an e-commerce catalog",
  attributeInfo,                              // describe your metadata
  structuredQueryTranslator: new HanaTranslator(),
});

await retriever.invoke("Show me AudioMax electronics under $400 with good ratings");

Behind the scenes, the LLM emits:

{
  $and: [
    { brand: "AudioMax" },
    { category: "electronics" },
    { price:  { $lt:  400 } },
    { rating: { $gte: 4 } }
  ]
}

Natural language in. Structured filter out. Vector search on top. Zero glue code.

There’s more in the box

What you’ve seen so far is the foundation. The library ships with three more capabilities, each big enough to deserve its own deep-dive:

  • Cross-encoding reranking with HanaReranker. Re-score your top candidates for surgical-precision results, the secret sauce behind great RAG and customer-facing search.
  • Knowledge graphs & SPARQL with HanaRdfGraph. Query RDF data, build natural-language Q&A over relationships, and combine graph reasoning with vector search.
  • Performance at scale with Map Merge, HNSW indexes, and specific metadata columns. Roughly 9× faster bulk inserts, sub-second search at million-document scale, and faster filtered queries.

Stay tuned for more updates and deep dives into each of these capabilities!

Get the code: SAP/langchainjs-integration-for-sap-hana-cloud  |  Read the full guide: blog-post.md

entry.title: Calculation View Features of 2026 QRC2
entry.content:
Within the time frame of 2026 Q2, several new calculation view features have been released in SAP Business Application Studio when connected to SAP HANA Cloud database QRC2. Some of these features are highlighted below. You can find examples that illustrate the individual features here. An overview of features of other releases can be found here.

Optimized Redeployment

When a calculation view is redeployed in a stacked scenario, all dependent objects in the same HDI container are normally dropped and sequentially recreated. This default behavior avoids cascading revalidations but comes with a cost: privileges on dependent objects are temporarily, or in cross-container scenarios potentially permanently, revoked, and the full recreate cycle adds significant deployment time.

Optimized redeployment addresses this. Instead of dropping all dependents upfront, a consistency check determines which dependent views are still valid after the change. Views unaffected by the redeployed calculation view are kept as-is and skipped entirely. This reduces both the time spent recreating unaffected views and the privilege reassignment overhead.

To enable optimized redeployment for calculation views, add the following parameter to the hdi-deploy invocation in package.json:

"start": "node node_modules/@sap/hdi-deploy/deploy.js --parameter com.sap.hana.di.calculationview/optimized_redeploy=true"

A global parameter (optimized_redeploy=true) is also available and activates the behavior for all HDI object types that support it.

Replace Instead of Drop and Recreate During Redeployment

By default, calculation views are dropped and recreated during redeployment. Dropping a view revokes all privileges associated with it. Privileges defined in HDI roles of the same container are automatically regranted at the end of deployment, but direct privileges held by roles outside the current HDI container are permanently lost.

The new replace option substitutes the drop-and-recreate cycle with an in-place replacement. Because the object is never dropped, existing privileges are preserved throughout deployment. The benefit is most significant when combined with optimized redeployment: without it, dependent views are dropped before the root view is replaced, so they never benefit from the replace logic. With both options active, the replace strategy also applies to dependent views.

To enable the replace behavior, pass the following parameter:

"start": "node node_modules/@sap/hdi-deploy/deploy.js --parameter optimized_replace=true"

Combining optimized_replace=true with optimized_redeploy=true gives the strongest deployment time and privilege preservation benefit.

 

Active/Active Read-Enabled Hints

In an Active/Active (Read-Enabled) setup, query routing hints can now be configured at the individual calculation view level. This gives fine-grained control over whether a query is directed to the primary system or the read replica, allowing workload to be distributed and overall throughput to be increased.

 

Hints are evaluated only on calculation views that are directly referenced in the query. 

 

Switching from a Join Node to a Non-Equi Join Node

A Join node can now be converted directly to a Non-Equi Join node via the node's context menu, without needing to manually rebuild the join logic.

During conversion you can choose how the resulting join condition is expressed: either graphically, based on the column display,

or as an explicit join expression.

This simplifies the refactoring of join logic and reduces manual rework. Note that features not supported by Non-Equi Join nodes, such as dynamic joins, text joins, and filter expressions, are removed automatically during the conversion.

Calculation View Status Dialog

When opening a calculation view with certain properties, a status dialog now appears and reports details about the current view state. Where applicable, the dialog also offers direct actions to resolve issues, for example, prompting you to log in to Cloud Foundry when no active database connection is found.

The dialog covers four categories:

  • Read-only mode: reasons why the calculation view was opened in read-only mode
  • Database connection: current connection status to the database
  • Data classification inconsistencies: detected mismatches in the data classification setting
  • BDC integration: whether the calculation view is used with BDC integration mode

 

Calculated Measures in MDS Cubes

Calculated measures can now be added to MDS Cubes and are evaluated at query runtime. This makes it possible to execute calculations at query runtime.

If a calculated measure cannot be translated into a valid MDS formula, deployment fails with a corresponding error message.

entry.title: Media Handling in SAP CAP: Mixing Static and Uploaded Images in Fiori Elements V4
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

When building applications with the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model and SAP Fiori Elements V4, handling media files is a common requirement. Usually, it is straightforward: first define a LargeBinary field, and the framework generates an upload control.

However, things get complicated when we need a single table column to dynamically display images from two different sources:

  1. Static default images (loaded via CSV during deployment).

  2. Custom images uploaded by users via the UI.

This guide covers the complete end-to-end setup to achieve this:

  • Data Modeling — Defining the schema with a "Traffic Cop" virtual field.

  • UI Annotations — Exposing the right fields to the List Report and Object Page.

  • Backend Logic — Intercepting OData V4 requests in Node.js to bypass UI optimizations.

  • HANA Deployment — Resolving common driver version conflicts during deployment.

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was developing an application on the SAP Business Technology Platform where users needed to browse a catalog of items. Some items had static placeholder images mapped to local CSV paths, while others had high-res photos explicitly uploaded by users.

When I attempted to route these dynamically using a virtual field, my Fiori V4 List Report stubbornly displayed blank "No Image" placeholders. After hours of debugging the OData V4 network payload, I realized Fiori was optimizing my request by stripping out the hidden columns my backend logic relied on. I couldn't find a definitive guide covering this specific Fiori V4 optimization trap, so I'm sharing the bulletproof workaround I built.

 

First, Let's Understand the Fiori V4 Optimization Trap

Before looking at the code, it helps to understand why the standard approach fails in Fiori Elements V4.

Fiori V4 is aggressively optimized for bandwidth. When it loads a List Report, the OData $select query only asks for the columns explicitly visible on the screen. If the backend relies on a virtual field (e.g., displayImageUrl) that calculates its value based on hidden fields (like mediaType or ImageUrl), those hidden fields arrive at the backend handler as undefined. We cannot calculate a dynamic URL if the framework refuses to fetch the underlying data.

To fix this, we don't fight the UI. Instead, we let Fiori request whatever it wants, and we use a custom CAP backend hook to silently query the database for the missing fields before delivering the final payload to the browser.

 

Architecture Overview

This solution relies on a clean separation between the database schema, the UI annotations, and the backend Node.js service.

project-root/
│
├── db/
│   ├── data/
│   │   └── mediahandling.db-Image.csv  ← Initial data linking to static image paths
│   └── schema.cds                      ← Defines the binary, static, and virtual fields
│
├── app/
│   ├── mediahandling/                  ← Your Fiori Elements application
│   │   └── webapp/
│   │       └── images/                 
│   │           ├── image1.jpg          ← The actual static placeholder files
│   │           └── image2.jpg
